proposals.title: A Modular Impedance-Based Stability Quantification Framework for Power Systems with High-Penetration Electric Vehicle Integration
proposals.motivation: The rapid, large-scale integration of electric vehicles (EVs) introduces highly stochastic, bidirectional power flows and power-electronic-dominated dynamics into power systems, posing significant and poorly quantified risks to voltage and small-signal stability. Existing stability studies often treat EV fleets as aggregated static loads or sources, failing to capture their complex, networked interactions with the grid and communication-dependent control delays, which are critical for accurate stability assessment in modern power-communication networks.
proposals.hypothesis: The stability impact of large-scale EV integration can be rigorously quantified by modeling the EV-grid system as a cascaded, multi-converter network, where the aggregate impedance seen from the grid side becomes a function of EV penetration patterns, charging control strategies, and underlying communication latencies, directly linking these factors to key stability metrics.
proposals.method: We will develop a detailed, modular impedance model for EV charging stations, building on Guerrero's prior work on cascaded systems in more-electric aircraft. This model will integrate grid-side converters, local energy storage, and multiple EV chargers. We will employ an electrical circuit analogy, similar to the researcher's data center work, to incorporate communication network latency models into the control loops of coordinated EV charging strategies. Stability will be analyzed by applying the Nyquist criterion to the derived aggregated impedance ratios, and the impact on stability margins will be quantified through parametric sweeps of EV penetration levels, charging power profiles, and latency bounds.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, scalable modeling framework to directly compute stability margins (e.g., gain and phase margins) as a function of EV integration scenarios. It will deliver actionable metrics for system planners to define safe hosting capacities for EVs and inform the design of stability-aware charging control protocols, thereby bridging the gap between high-level energy policy studies and detailed power-electronic stability analysis.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of modular impedance modeling for cascaded power-electronic systems with communication latency analysis from cyber-physical networks, creating an integrated model specifically tailored to quantify the stability impact of stochastic, communication-dependent EV fleets, a synthesis not present in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Multi-Agent Control Framework for Quantifying the Resilience Contribution of Electric Vehicle Fleets in Islanded Microgrids
proposals.motivation: The increasing frequency of extreme weather events and grid disturbances underscores the need for resilient, self-sufficient microgrids. Electric vehicle (EV) fleets represent a significant, mobile, and underutilized distributed energy resource (DER) that could provide critical backup power and grid services during outages. However, a systematic, quantitative assessment of how coordinated EV fleets can enhance microgrid survivability and recovery, considering both power and communication constraints, is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-agent control framework, coordinating EV fleets as dynamic, aggregated storage, can significantly enhance the transient stability and long-term survivability of islanded microgrids during outages, and that this contribution can be quantified via a novel resilience metric integrating power availability and communication latency.
proposals.method: We will develop a modular impedance-based model of the islanded microgrid, integrating EV fleets as aggregated, controllable sources/sinks. A multi-agent system (MAS) control architecture, inspired by Guerrero's prior work on hierarchical control, will be implemented to coordinate primary (droop) and secondary (restoration) control actions among EVs and other DERs. The framework's performance will be tested under N-1 and N-k contingency scenarios using real-time digital simulation (RTDS). A key analysis will adapt the circuit analogy-based maximum latency calculation method to model the impact of communication delays on the stability and resilience of the coordinated power-communication network.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, quantitative framework for assessing the resilience value of EV fleets, moving beyond qualitative potential. It will deliver a novel co-simulation platform integrating detailed power system dynamics with communication network models to evaluate cyber-physical interdependencies. The results will offer actionable insights for microgrid planners and aggregators on sizing, siting, and controlling EV fleets for resilience.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of communication network latency constraints, modeled via an electrical circuit analogy, into the dynamic stability and resilience assessment of EV-supported microgrids. This creates a unified cyber-physical evaluation tool not present in existing studies that treat EV control and grid stability separately.

proposals.title: A Hierarchical Droop-Communication Control Architecture for Single-Home Resilience Using an Electric Vehicle as a Standalone Microgrid
proposals.motivation: Increasing grid outages from extreme weather and aging infrastructure threaten residential energy security. While electric vehicles (EVs) have significant battery capacity, existing vehicle-to-home (V2H) systems often rely on stable grid signals or complex communication, failing in complete blackouts. A robust control system is needed to autonomously manage a single EV as a standalone microgrid for a single home, ensuring stability without external references.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid control architecture combining local, communication-free droop control for primary voltage/frequency stability with a low-bandwidth secondary layer for power sharing and state-of-charge management can reliably operate an EV as an islanded single-home microgrid, maintaining power quality and extending backup duration.
proposals.method: We will design a hierarchical controller. The primary layer uses modular impedance-based modeling, inspired by Guerrero's aircraft work, to design robust V/f droop control for the EV's bidirectional inverter, ensuring stability with highly variable home loads. A secondary layer, using an event-triggered consensus algorithm over a low-rate communication network (e.g., WiFi/Zigbee), will adjust droop setpoints to manage the EV's state-of-charge and prioritize critical loads. Performance will be validated via hardware-in-the-loop simulation, testing against standard home load profiles and outage scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel control framework for ultra-localized, resilient energy systems, extending microgrid concepts to the single-home, single-source scale. It will deliver a practical control design methodology for ensuring power quality and resource management in a communication-degraded environment, contributing directly to distributed grid stability.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely scales microgrid control architectures down to a single distributed energy resource (the EV) and a single load center (the home), a problem distinct from multi-source microgrids. It specifically addresses the black-start and islanded stability challenge for V2H, which is under-explored compared to grid-connected ancillary services.

proposals.title: A Cyber-Physical Co-Design Framework for Future Distribution Grids Enabling Ubiquitous Vehicle-to-Grid Services
proposals.motivation: Current grid architectures are being retrofitted for Electric Vehicle (EV) integration, treating V2G as an ancillary service. This reactive approach fails to capture the transformative potential of ubiquitous, bidirectional EV fleets as primary grid assets. A proactive, inverse design is needed to unlock systemic benefits in stability, resilience, and renewable hosting capacity, moving beyond localized congestion management.
proposals.hypothesis: A distribution grid architecture co-designed from first principles with ubiquitous V2G as a core constraint will yield superior technical and economic performance compared to incrementally adapted legacy systems, by fundamentally re-optimizing the power-electronics-control-communication nexus.
proposals.method: We will develop a modular impedance-based modeling framework, inspired by Guerrero's work on cascaded systems in more-electric aircraft, to represent high-penetration V2G clusters as active subsystems. This model will be integrated into a multi-objective optimization problem minimizing infrastructure cost while maximizing stability margins and V2G service availability. The optimization will use a genetic algorithm to inversely design grid topology, power converter specifications, and distributed control parameters (e.g., for primary voltage support) simultaneously. The cyber-layer will be co-designed using a latency calculation method analogous to the cited data center work to ensure communication constraints meet real-time control needs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, open-source cyber-physical co-design tool for future-proof grid planning. It will deliver specific architectural blueprints (e.g., for feeder segmentation, power-hardware-in-the-loop converter specs, and control hierarchies) that maximize V2G value. The results will establish quantitative performance benchmarks for next-generation distribution systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the inverse, first-principles design approach and the explicit co-design of power, control, and communication layers for V2G, rather than treating EVs as an afterthought. It uniquely applies modular stability analysis from aerospace systems to the ground-based grid domain to ensure dynamic robustness.

proposals.title: A Fleet-Level Battery Health-Aware Dispatch Framework for Electric Vehicle Grid Services
proposals.motivation: The large-scale integration of electric vehicles (EVs) for grid services (e.g., frequency regulation, peak shaving) presents a dual challenge: optimizing grid stability while managing the economic impact of battery degradation on EV owners. Existing Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) optimization primarily minimizes immediate grid operational costs, often treating battery wear as a simplified, individual constraint. This overlooks the aggregate, long-term degradation across the fleet, which could deter participation and undermine grid resilience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a fleet-level dispatch strategy, which explicitly minimizes aggregate battery degradation as a primary objective, can maintain required grid performance while significantly extending the useful life of the EV fleet compared to cost-only optimization, creating a more sustainable V2G ecosystem.
proposals.method: The method builds on Guerrero's expertise in modular impedance modeling and stability analysis. First, we develop a high-fidelity, semi-empirical battery degradation model (incorporating C-rate, depth-of-discharge, and temperature) suitable for grid-dispatch timescales. Second, we formulate a dual-objective optimization problem minimizing a weighted sum of grid service error and total fleet degradation. Third, we propose a distributed control architecture, inspired by multi-agent microgrid control, where an aggregator solves the fleet-level problem and broadcasts dispatch signals, while local controllers enforce individual battery health constraints using model predictive control (MPC). Stability of this cascaded control loop will be analyzed using impedance-based methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel fleet-centric optimization framework that internalizes battery health as a system-wide resource, 2) a practical, stability-guaranteed control architecture for its implementation, and 3) quantitative evidence of the long-term trade-offs between grid service quality and fleet battery longevity, informing future market and policy design.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in reframing the V2G dispatch problem from minimizing grid cost with degradation constraints to directly minimizing aggregate degradation as a system objective, and in integrating detailed, physics-informed battery aging models into a real-time grid stability control framework.

proposals.title: A Comparative Methodology for Short-Term Renewable Generation Prediction in Islanded Microgrids Using Multi-Modal Data Fusion
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of distributed renewable energy resources at the microgrid and building level increases operational complexity, especially for islanded systems. Accurate short-term generation prediction is critical for maintaining power quality, stability, and energy management, but existing large-grid forecasting methods often fail to capture the high volatility and limited inertia of small-scale systems. This gap hinders the reliable and optimal control of decentralized energy systems, a core challenge in modern power electronics-based grids.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a comparative methodology that fuses local power measurements with exogenous data streams (e.g., weather, occupancy information) can significantly improve the prediction accuracy of renewable generation for a single microgrid compared to models using only historical power data. This enhanced prediction will directly improve the performance of model-predictive control strategies for voltage and frequency stability.
proposals.method: The research will adapt the comparative, multi-model analysis framework from Guerrero's prior work on network latency and system stability to the generation forecasting problem. Specifically, we will develop and compare three prediction architectures: 1) a physics-informed grey-box model based on modular impedance concepts, 2) a data-driven model (e.g., LSTM or GRU) trained on local SCADA and weather data, and 3) a hybrid model integrating both. Performance will be evaluated using real data from a laboratory-scale islanded AC microgrid, with metrics including RMSE and its impact on a co-simulated predictive energy management system.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, scalable framework for high-accuracy renewable forecasting tailored to the dynamics of islanded microgrids. It will contribute a concrete methodology for fusing cyber (information) and physical (power) system data to enhance predictive control, directly addressing stability concerns in more-electric aircraft and naval shipboard power systems. The results will offer practical guidelines for selecting prediction models based on microgrid configuration and available data.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct application of a comparative, cross-domain methodology—previously used for network latency and modular stability analysis—to the specific problem of microgrid-level generation forecasting. This approach systematically evaluates how different model architectures and data sources perform in the context of a tightly coupled power-information system, which is non-trivial given the unique noise and coupling characteristics at this scale.

proposals.title: A Cyber-Physical Co-Simulation Framework for Multi-Timescale Stability and Carbon-Aware Energy Exchange Optimization in the Turkey-Balkan Power Corridor
proposals.motivation: The European energy transition necessitates increased cross-border renewable energy exchange, yet the Turkey-Balkan corridor presents challenges of asynchronous grids, volatile generation, and disparate carbon pricing policies. Pre-2026, optimizing such multinational coordination requires a unified framework that simultaneously addresses dynamic grid stability, communication latency impacts, and low-carbon economic dispatch, which existing siloed approaches fail to provide.
proposals.hypothesis: A co-simulation framework integrating modular impedance-based stability analysis, carbon-aware optimal power flow, and data-center-like latency modeling can significantly improve the secure and economically optimal volume of renewable energy exchange across asynchronous multinational grids.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical, multi-timescale cyber-physical co-simulation platform. For fast-timescale AC bus stability, we extend Guerrero's modular impedance modeling to characterize the cascaded interaction of HVDC links and asynchronous AC grids. For slower dispatch timescales, we formulate a stochastic optimization problem, integrating carbon pricing and energy storage, solved via a distributed ADMM algorithm to respect national autonomy. The framework will incorporate a latency calculation module, inspired by the electrical circuit analogy for data centers, to assess the impact of communication delays on control signals and market clearing.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, validated co-simulation tool for multinational grid operators, enabling joint stability-economic analysis. It will provide specific policy and operational guidelines for increasing renewable exchange in the Turkey-Balkan region while maintaining system security. The research will advance the field of cyber-physical power systems by formally coupling impedance-based stability domains with market and communication-layer models.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight integration of Guerrero's core methods—modular impedance stability and latency-aware cyber modeling—with carbon-constrained economic dispatch into a single cross-border coordination framework, a synthesis not previously attempted for asynchronous multinational grids.

proposals.title: Inverse Planning Framework for Renewable Capacity in Microgrids: A Constrained Optimization Approach Integrating Power-Communication Latency and Stability Metrics
proposals.motivation: The global transition to renewable energy necessitates precise capacity planning to meet decarbonization targets while ensuring grid stability. Existing forward models predict output from given capacity, but planners urgently need inverse methods to determine the required capacity for a desired, reliable energy output, especially in complex systems like microgrids where communication delays and power-electronic interactions impact performance.
proposals.hypothesis: An inverse optimization framework, incorporating power-communication network latency constraints and modular impedance stability criteria, can accurately determine the minimum required renewable generation and storage capacity to meet a specified, time-varying energy output profile with guaranteed system stability.
proposals.method: We will formulate a constrained inverse optimization problem. The core is an objective function minimizing capital cost of wind/PV/battery capacity, subject to constraints derived from inverting Guerrero's team's forward models: 1) a modified low-carbon dispatch model to ensure target output under carbon pricing, 2) impedance-based stability margins from cascaded converter models to guarantee AC bus voltage stability, and 3) maximum latency constraints from power-communication co-simulation to ensure control viability. The problem will be solved using a hybrid metaheuristic (e.g., Genetic Algorithm) and sequential quadratic programming to handle non-convexities.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic, stability-aware tool for microgrid and distribution system planners to translate energy output targets into concrete, feasible technology portfolios. It will formally bridge forward performance prediction and inverse system design within Guerrero's research themes, contributing a new methodology for cyber-physical power system planning.
proposals.novelty: The inverse formulation explicitly integrates dynamic communication latency bounds and small-signal stability limits from modular impedance models as constraints, moving beyond simple energy balance to ensure the planned capacity is both sufficient and dynamically viable, which is not addressed in existing capacity expansion literature.

proposals.title: A Modular Impedance-Based Stability and Control Strategy for Islanded Microgrids Under Extreme Renewable Intermittency
proposals.motivation: The increasing penetration of highly intermittent solar and wind generation in islanded microgrids poses severe challenges to voltage and frequency stability. Traditional droop control and centralized strategies struggle to maintain performance during rapid, large-magnitude power fluctuations, risking system collapse. A robust, decentralized control framework is urgently needed to ensure reliable operation under these extreme conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a modular impedance modeling and analysis framework, analogous to methods used in more-electric aircraft cascaded systems, will enable the design of a decentralized control strategy that can guarantee small-signal stability and maintain voltage regulation in microgrids, even under scenarios of extreme renewable generation intermittency.
proposals.method: The research will adapt the modular impedance modeling technique from Guerrero's recent work on more-electric aircraft. Each distributed energy resource (DER), including inverters for PV and wind, will be characterized by its output impedance. The overall microgrid network impedance will be constructed. Stability will be assessed using the Nyquist criterion based on the impedance ratio. A decentralized control strategy, integrating adaptive virtual impedance and consensus-based secondary correction, will be designed and tuned using this model. Performance will be tested via real-time simulation (e.g., OPAL-RT) with historical high-variability solar/wind profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic, physics-based design methodology for stable microgrid control under high renewable penetration, moving beyond trial-and-error approaches. It will deliver a validated, decentralized control strategy that enhances resilience to intermittency, contributing directly to the low-carbon optimization of future power systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the cross-domain application of a rigorous modular impedance stability analysis—previously focused on tightly coupled aircraft systems—to the geographically dispersed and highly intermittent environment of renewable-rich microgrids, and the subsequent synthesis of a tailored decentralized control law based on this analysis.

proposals.title: A Distributed, Cost-Aware Secondary Control Framework for Islanded Microgrids: From Proportional Sharing to Economic Dispatch
proposals.motivation: In islanded AC microgrids, conventional secondary control often enforces proportional power sharing based on inverter ratings, ignoring the heterogeneous and time-varying generation costs of distributed energy resources (DERs). With the increasing penetration of renewable and storage systems, this approach leads to suboptimal economic performance. A control paradigm shift is needed to integrate real-time economic dispatch objectives directly into the distributed control layer, enhancing operational efficiency without compromising stability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by reformulating the secondary control objective using a distributed consensus-based algorithm that incorporates incremental cost as a consensus variable, an islanded microgrid can achieve a stable operating point that satisfies both frequency/voltage regulation and optimal economic dispatch.
proposals.method: We will extend a conventional distributed-averaging proportional-integral (DAPI) secondary control architecture. The consensus protocol will be modified to drive the incremental costs (λ_i = dC_i(P_i)/dP_i) of all dispatchable DERs to equality, satisfying the Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) conditions for economic dispatch. A primal-dual gradient algorithm will be implemented in a distributed manner, using only neighbor-to-neighbor communication. Stability will be analyzed using modular impedance models and small-signal techniques, building on Guerrero's prior work in cascaded systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete control framework that seamlessly integrates economic optimization into the real-time control layer of a microgrid. It will deliver a rigorous stability analysis for the proposed cost-aware controller and validate its performance against traditional proportional sharing in hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) tests, demonstrating significant cost reduction.
proposals.novelty: While economic dispatch is well-studied at the grid level, its integration as the primary objective of a distributed, plug-and-play secondary control loop for islanded microgrids is novel. The key innovation is the direct modification of the consensus variable from power mismatch to incremental cost, creating a true dual control problem.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Data-Driven Economic Model Predictive Control Framework for Enhanced Stability in Renewable-Dominant Microgrids
proposals.motivation: The high penetration of volatile renewable energy sources and unpredictable loads introduces significant forecasting uncertainties into microgrid operations. Traditional Economic Model Predictive Control (EMPC) relies on deterministic or simplistic stochastic forecasts, which can compromise system stability and economic performance. A robust, adaptive control framework is urgently needed to ensure reliable and optimal grid operation under uncertainty.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a machine learning-based uncertainty quantification module directly into the EMPC optimization loop will yield more robust and economically efficient dispatch decisions, thereby enhancing voltage stability and overall system performance compared to conventional EMPC.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage hybrid framework. First, a deep ensemble of Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks will generate multi-step-ahead probabilistic forecasts for renewable generation and load, quantifying prediction intervals. Second, these uncertainty sets will be embedded as time-varying, chance-like constraints within a distributed EMPC formulation for a multi-bus AC microgrid. The EMPC's finite-horizon optimal power flow problem will be solved using a real-time iteration scheme, with the ML module updated online using streaming data from phasor measurement units.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, practically implementable control architecture that tightly couples data-driven forecasting with optimal control. It will provide a methodological advance for handling uncertainty in EMPC and deliver concrete case studies demonstrating improved voltage stability margins and reduced operational costs in islanded microgrids.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the closed-loop, online integration of a deep ensemble forecast model directly into the EMPC constraint formulation, moving beyond using ML for standalone forecasting. This addresses a key gap in making EMPC inherently adaptive to non-stationary uncertainty patterns in integrated power-communication networks.

proposals.title: A Unified Market-Oriented Control Framework for Multi-Energy Microgrids Integrating Diverse Power-to-X and Storage Technologies
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of distributed energy resources (DERs) extends beyond conventional batteries and renewables to include emerging power-to-X (P2X) devices like electrolyzers, fuel cells, and thermal storage. Existing market and control models, often designed for traditional assets, struggle to capture the diverse dynamic constraints, conversion efficiencies, and coupled energy vectors of these technologies. This limits their optimal participation in grid services and hinders the economic viability of integrated multi-energy systems.
proposals.hypothesis: A modular, impedance-inspired modeling framework, integrated with a hierarchical market-clearing mechanism, can effectively internalize the distinct operational dynamics of diverse P2X and storage devices, thereby enhancing overall system stability and economic performance in a multi-energy microgrid.
proposals.method: We will first develop a unified modular impedance model for each device class (e.g., electrolyzer, flywheel, CAES), extending Guerrero's prior work on cascaded systems to represent their port characteristics across electrical, thermal, and chemical domains. These models will inform constraint formulation for a two-layer optimization: a local device-level model predictive control (MPC) for dynamic stability and a community-level transactive energy market using an alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM) for energy and ancillary service dispatch. The framework's performance will be validated via hardware-in-the-loop simulation of a laboratory-scale microgrid.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable methodological framework for the techno-economic co-optimization of heterogeneous energy conversion assets in microgrids. It will deliver specific stability criteria for systems with high penetration of P2X technologies and demonstrate a practical market mechanism to unlock their aggregated grid-support potential.
proposals.novelty: The integration of device-level modular impedance models (for stability) with system-level transactive energy markets (for economics) for multi-energy P2X assets is a novel synthesis, moving beyond siloed analyses of either stability or markets for simpler DERs.

proposals.title: A Carbon-First Design Framework: Modular Hybrid Converter Topologies for Cell-Level Carbon-Aware Battery Management in Grid Applications
proposals.motivation: The decarbonization of power systems, driven by carbon pricing and high renewable penetration, necessitates energy storage that actively minimizes its operational carbon footprint. Existing battery management systems (BMS) and power converters prioritize efficiency, cost, and battery health, treating carbon intensity as a secondary, software-level constraint. This creates a fundamental hardware-software mismatch where the physical system is not architected for the primary objective of carbon minimization.
proposals.hypothesis: By inverting the design priority to place a strict operational carbon constraint first, novel hybrid power converter topologies can be synthesized to enable granular, cell-level state-of-charge (SoC) control, which in turn allows the battery system to function as a carbon-aware asset, dynamically aligning its energy state with the grid's carbon intensity for a net reduction in emissions.
proposals.method: The research will first define a carbon-minimization objective function, constrained by battery longevity and grid stability metrics. A modular, cascaded hybrid converter architecture, inspired by impedance modeling techniques for more-electric aircraft, will be designed. This hardware enables independent, cell-level power flow control. We will develop a hierarchical control algorithm where a primary layer uses real-time carbon intensity information to set a system-wide carbon-aware power setpoint, and a secondary layer performs optimal cell-level SoC balancing using the converter's granular control to execute the setpoint while minimizing internal losses and degradation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a new 'carbon-first' hardware-software co-design methodology for grid-connected storage, 2) a specific hybrid converter topology enabling cell-level carbon-aware operation, and 3) a quantitative framework analyzing the trade-off between carbon savings, battery degradation, and economic cost under carbon pricing mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in fundamentally inverting the design paradigm—treating carbon as a primary hardware design constraint rather than a secondary software objective—and in the synthesis of hybrid converter structures specifically to exploit cell-level SoC granularity for carbon optimization, not just battery health.

proposals.title: Synergistic Co-Optimization of Wind-Solar Generation and Multi-Temporal Demand Flexibility for Enhanced Grid Stability
proposals.motivation: The high penetration of variable renewable energy sources (VRES) like wind and solar introduces significant challenges for power system stability and operational efficiency, often leading to curtailment and reliance on conventional peaking plants. Existing demand-side management strategies frequently treat generation and load flexibility as separate, additive resources, failing to exploit the complementary temporal patterns of wind and solar availability. This research addresses the critical need for an integrated scheduling framework that actively matches the distinct diurnal and seasonal profiles of a multi-renewable portfolio with temporally appropriate flexible loads to enhance grid stability and economic performance.
proposals.hypothesis: A co-optimized scheduling model that explicitly leverages the complementary temporal characteristics of wind and solar generation to target specific, heterogeneous demand-side response strategies will significantly reduce renewable curtailment and peaking plant dependency, thereby improving overall system stability at high VRES penetration levels.
proposals.method: We will develop a mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) model for day-ahead scheduling that integrates probabilistic forecasts for wind and solar generation. The model will incorporate a detailed taxonomy of flexible loads (e.g., deferrable, interruptible, thermal storage) with quantified temporal flexibility constraints. The core innovation is the formulation of temporal matching constraints that align solar over-generation periods with daytime deferrable loads and utilize nighttime wind power for thermal pre-charging of buildings and water heaters. System stability will be evaluated using modular impedance-based analysis, extending Dr. Guerrero's prior work on cascaded systems, to assess the impact of the proposed schedule on AC bus voltage stability in the distribution network.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, synergistic scheduling framework that moves beyond simple aggregation of resources. It will deliver a practical tool for system operators to minimize curtailment and operational costs while explicitly considering stability metrics. The research will also contribute new insights into how the inherent variability of a multi-renewable portfolio can be transformed into a grid-stabilizing asset through intelligent demand-side coordination.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit, constraint-based temporal matching between the distinct generation patterns of a combined wind-solar portfolio and heterogeneous demand-side resources, rather than treating flexibility as a generic, time-shiftable energy block. This creates a non-additive, synergistic optimization where the portfolio's variability directly informs and targets specific response strategies.

proposals.title: Distributionally Robust Co-Optimization of Energy and Reserves for Power Systems with Spatio-Temporally Correlated Renewables Using Copula-Based Risk Modeling
proposals.motivation: The increasing penetration of wind and solar generation introduces significant volatility and compound weather risks, such as continent-wide 'dunkelflaute' events, which threaten grid stability and reliability. Existing scheduling models often treat renewable forecasts as independent or use simplistic uncertainty sets, failing to capture the complex spatio-temporal correlations driven by large-scale meteorological patterns, leading to inefficient or insecure dispatch.
proposals.hypothesis: A power system scheduling framework that explicitly models the multivariate, spatio-temporal dependence structure of wind and solar forecast errors using copula theory will enable a distributionally robust optimization (DRO) formulation that yields more reliable and cost-effective generation and reserve schedules against correlated renewable shortfalls.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage data-driven model. First, a Gaussian copula model will be constructed to capture the non-Gaussian marginal distributions and complex dependence of historical wind/solar forecast errors across multiple nodes. Second, this probabilistic model will inform an ambiguity set for a distributionally robust chance-constrained optimal power flow (OPF) problem, co-optimizing energy dispatch and reserves. The DRO problem will be solved using a cutting-plane or affine policy approximation, building on pre-2026 advances in tractable robust optimization for microgrids and cascaded systems, akin to Guerrero's prior work on stability analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, data-driven framework for quantifying and hedging against compound weather risks in system scheduling. It will advance the field of resilient grid operation by delivering a practical tool for system operators to transition from deterministic or simple stochastic models to correlation-aware, risk-averse scheduling, directly enhancing stability in high-renewable penetration scenarios.
proposals.novelty: The integration of copula-based multivariate probabilistic forecasting directly into a distributionally robust OPF formulation for joint energy-reserve scheduling is novel, moving beyond univariate or correlation-ignorant uncertainty sets to explicitly manage the risk of spatially widespread renewable generation deficits.

proposals.title: A Data Assimilation Framework for Multi-Source Renewable Forecast Integration in Security-Constrained Power System Scheduling
proposals.motivation: The integration of intermittent wind and solar PV generation poses a fundamental challenge to power system stability and economic dispatch due to their forecast uncertainty. Current scheduling models often treat wind, solar, and load forecasts as separate, deterministic inputs or use simplistic uncertainty sets, ignoring their complex spatial and temporal correlations. This leads to either overly conservative or risky operational schedules, increasing costs or threatening grid security.
proposals.hypothesis: By formally treating short-term renewable and load forecasts as heterogeneous, error-prone observations analogous to weather data, a data assimilation framework can optimally blend them to produce a more accurate and statistically consistent input scenario (or ensemble) for scheduling, thereby improving economic and operational performance.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Ensemble Kalman Filter (EnKF), a proven NWP technique, to the power system domain. Short-term forecasts for wind, solar PV, and load will be treated as ensemble observations with error covariance matrices derived from historical data, capturing source-specific uncertainties and cross-correlations. This framework will be integrated into a modular AC optimal power flow (OPF) architecture, similar to Guerrero's prior work on impedance modeling, to solve a security-constrained unit commitment problem where the assimilated forecast ensemble defines the scenario constraints.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, physics-inspired methodology for multi-renewable forecast integration directly into grid scheduling models. It will deliver a concrete algorithmic tool to improve schedule robustness and reduce operational costs, contributing to the low-carbon optimization of modern power systems. The research will also bridge concepts between numerical weather prediction and power system control.
proposals.novelty: The novel application of NWP data assimilation techniques to the problem of blending correlated wind, solar, and load forecasts for grid scheduling is non-obvious. It moves beyond treating forecasts as independent inputs or using generic uncertainty sets by explicitly modeling their joint error statistics within a rigorous estimation framework.

proposals.title: A Stochastic Co-Optimization Framework for Integrated Wind-Solar Power Systems with Carbon Capture and Storage Under Grid Stability Constraints
proposals.motivation: The decarbonization of power systems relies heavily on variable renewable energy (VRE) sources like wind and solar. Pre-2026, optimal scheduling models often focus on single VRE types, neglecting the complementary generation profiles and distinct grid integration challenges of hybrid wind-solar plants. This gap limits the ability to fully leverage their combined potential for reliable, low-carbon power while maintaining system stability under stringent carbon pricing mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating detailed, stochastic models of solar photovoltaic generation into an existing wind-CCS-ES scheduling framework will yield a more cost-effective and stable dispatch strategy by exploiting temporal complementarity, thereby reducing operational costs and enhancing voltage stability margins compared to single-source VRE models.
proposals.method: We will extend the low-carbon optimization model from Guerrero's recent work by incorporating a stochastic unit commitment formulation. Solar generation will be modeled using historical irradiance data to create probabilistic forecasts, integrated as correlated uncertainty scenarios with wind power. The method will employ a two-stage stochastic programming approach, solved via a modified Benders decomposition, to co-optimize energy from wind-solar hybrids, carbon capture plant operation, and energy storage dispatch. AC bus voltage stability constraints, informed by modular impedance modeling techniques from the aircraft power system research, will be incorporated as linearized security constraints within the scheduling optimization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, holistic scheduling tool for hybrid renewable power plants co-located with carbon management infrastructure. It will deliver quantifiable insights into the economic and stability benefits of wind-solar complementarity under carbon policies. The integrated model will also offer a framework for analyzing the trade-offs between carbon reduction, cost, and grid voltage stability in future high-VRE penetration scenarios.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty lies in the explicit integration of solar PV stochasticity and grid stability constraints, derived from impedance-based analysis, into a unified wind-CCS-ES co-optimization model, creating a cross-domain synthesis of Guerrero's team's recent methodological advances in carbon pricing optimization and stability analysis.

proposals.title: Adversarial Scenario Ensemble Framework for Robust Validation of Grid Optimization Models Under High-Penetration Renewables
proposals.motivation: Modern power system optimization models for grid stability and low-carbon operation are typically validated against limited historical data, which may not encompass future high-stress conditions. With the increasing penetration of variable renewable energy sources, the grid faces novel risks such as prolonged renewable droughts and spatially correlated failures of power-electronic interfaces, which historical data does not adequately represent. This creates a critical gap in model trustworthiness for planning and real-time control.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that validating a grid optimization model's performance and identifying its failure boundaries across a synthetically generated ensemble of adversarial, physically-consistent future scenarios provides a more robust guarantee of real-world reliability than validation against historical data alone.
proposals.method: The framework first constructs statistical and physics-based models (e.g., modular impedance models for power-electronic systems, copula models for correlated resource failures) from historical data. It then employs adversarial optimization techniques, such as a Wasserstein Generative Adversarial Network (WGAN), to generate a wide ensemble of synthetic, high-stress scenarios that stress specific model vulnerabilities (e.g., voltage stability, latency in cyber-physical links). The core grid optimization model (e.g., a low-carbon dispatch with storage) is then evaluated across this ensemble, with performance metrics and failure modes systematically analyzed.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic, scenario-based validation methodology that moves beyond point estimates of model fit. It will deliver a concrete framework for power system planners to quantify model robustness and identify critical infrastructure vulnerabilities under extreme yet plausible futures. The approach directly enhances the reliability of optimization models used for stability analysis and low-carbon transition planning.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of adversarial machine learning for scenario generation with rigorous power system physics and stability models (e.g., modular impedance analysis) to create a targeted stress-testing environment. This moves beyond Monte Carlo simulation by actively searching for scenarios that challenge the optimization model's core assumptions and control strategies.

proposals.title: Backtesting for Power System Model Validation: An Epidemiological Analogy Applied to Grid Optimization
proposals.motivation: The increasing complexity of power systems, driven by high renewable penetration and market dynamics, demands highly accurate optimization models for planning and operation. However, model validation often relies on synthetic scenarios or limited benchmarks, lacking a rigorous, reality-grounded assessment of predictive fidelity. This gap risks poor operational decisions and undermines confidence in models used for critical infrastructure.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying an epidemiological backtesting methodology to a power system optimization model will quantitatively reveal its predictive accuracy and identify structural weaknesses when compared against high-fidelity historical data. This process will demonstrate that such retrospective validation is essential for calibrating model parameters and improving its reliability for forward-looking studies.
proposals.method: We will adapt a security-constrained unit commitment (SCUC) and economic dispatch (SCED) model, formulated as a mixed-integer linear program (MILP). Using historical data from a major ISO (e.g., ERCOT 2021), we will initialize the model with the actual system state and run it forward, feeding it the true sequence of load, renewable output, and outage events. The model's decisions (dispatch, LMPs) will be compared to archival records using metrics like mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) for prices and normalized dispatch deviation. Sensitivity analysis on key parameters (e.g., reserve margins, generator ramping) will be performed to minimize the discrepancy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative framework for power system model validation, moving beyond conventional scenario-based testing. It will deliver a calibrated, high-fidelity optimization model for the test system and a methodological template for other researchers. The results will directly inform model improvements, increasing trust in simulations used for grid planning with high renewables.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the systematic translation of the epidemiological backtesting paradigm—a gold standard in public health—to power systems engineering. While historical data is sometimes used for tuning, the rigorous, full-sequence simulation against the complete historical record, as proposed, is a non-obvious and formalized approach to holistic model validation.

proposals.title: Real-World Validation of a Low-Carbon Optimization Model for Integrated Wind Power and Carbon Capture Systems Using Historical Grid Data
proposals.motivation: The transition to low-carbon power systems, integrating high shares of variable renewables like wind, requires robust co-optimization of generation, storage, and carbon management technologies. Prior models, such as those proposed for wind-integrated systems with carbon capture and storage (CCS), are often validated only in simplified, synthetic networks, leaving their performance and stability in real, complex grid conditions uncertain. Validating these models with actual historical grid data is a critical step before their deployment can be trusted for operational planning and policy support.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a previously proposed low-carbon optimization model for wind-integrated systems with CCS and energy storage will demonstrate robust economic and stability performance when validated against real-world historical grid data, but will reveal specific, data-driven sensitivities to grid volatility and communication latencies not apparent in synthetic tests.
proposals.method: The method will utilize historical SCADA and market data from a real power system (e.g., the Danish grid, rich in wind penetration). The core optimization framework will be the previously proposed model integrating unit commitment, economic dispatch, carbon pricing, and CCS flexibility. We will implement a co-simulation platform coupling this power model with a communication network model, applying the Electrical Circuit Analogy-Based method to calculate data latency impacts on control signals. Performance will be assessed using metrics for cost, carbon intensity, and voltage stability, the latter analyzed via modular impedance methods akin to those used for aircraft cascaded systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a crucial validation bridge between theoretical low-carbon optimization models and practical grid implementation. It will yield a benchmarked, data-tested model and a set of case-specific insights on the interdependencies between economic dispatch, carbon management, and grid stability in a high-renewable context. The findings will inform system operators and planners about the real-world constraints and performance of integrated CCS and storage solutions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the rigorous, multi-fidelity validation of a coupled power-communication-carbon optimization model using comprehensive historical data from an actual high-renewable grid, explicitly testing the intersection of economic, low-carbon, and stability objectives under real volatility and latency conditions.

proposals.title: A Multi-Stage Stochastic Co-Optimization Framework for Generation, Transmission, and Multi-Technology Storage Portfolios in Deeply Decarbonized Power Systems
proposals.motivation: Achieving deep decarbonization requires massive integration of variable renewable energy (VRE), which challenges grid stability and reliability due to its intermittency. While energy storage is widely recognized as a key solution, existing planning models often treat storage generically or in isolation, failing to capture the distinct technical characteristics, cost structures, and value-stacking potential of diverse technologies like Li-ion, pumped hydro, and flow batteries. A systems-level co-optimization framework is urgently needed to reveal the cost-effective portfolio and operational roles of these technologies under long-term uncertainty.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a least-cost, reliable, and low-carbon generation-transmission portfolio critically depends on the explicit, technology-aware co-optimization of multiple storage types, and that this approach will reveal non-obvious complementarities and optimal market value allocations between storage technologies over multi-decade horizons.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-stage stochastic mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) model spanning a 30-year horizon with representative days for hourly operational fidelity. The model will co-optimize investment in generation (VRE, gas with CCS), transmission, and multiple storage technologies, each with distinct power-energy relationships, degradation, and efficiency models. It will incorporate value streams for energy arbitrage, capacity, and frequency regulation. The optimization will be solved using a decomposition algorithm (e.g., Progressive Hedging) to manage computational complexity from long-term uncertainty in fuel prices, technology costs, and VRE output.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, quantitative blueprint for the optimal sizing, timing, and operation of heterogeneous storage fleets in future grids. It will deliver novel insights into the market value evolution and competitive niches of different storage technologies, moving beyond simple LCOS comparisons. The resulting open-source framework will be a valuable tool for policymakers and system planners.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit, technology-differentiated modeling of multiple storage types within a unified, long-term stochastic co-optimization of generation, transmission, and storage, capturing their synergistic and competitive interactions under deep decarbonization constraints—a significant advancement over single-technology or myopic valuation studies.

proposals.title: A Multi-Timescale Stability and Economic Co-Optimization Framework for Hybrid Energy Storage Systems in Renewable-Dense Microgrids
proposals.motivation: The integration of variable renewable energy sources challenges microgrid stability and economic operation. While diverse energy storage technologies (e.g., lithium-ion batteries, flow batteries, pumped hydro) offer complementary characteristics in power density, energy capacity, and response time, their synergistic operation for both grid support and market participation remains sub-optimally defined. A systematic, stability-aware framework is needed to co-optimize the dispatch of heterogeneous storage assets across multiple timescales.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical control and scheduling framework, which explicitly models the dynamic impedance and latency characteristics of different storage technologies, can co-optimize for system stability and operational economics more effectively than single-technology or purely economic approaches.
proposals.method: The research will employ a modular impedance modeling approach, akin to Guerrero's work on cascaded systems, to create dynamic models for battery (fast, high-power) and pumped hydro/flow battery (slow, high-energy) systems. A two-layer optimization architecture will be developed: a lower layer uses distributed consensus control for fast voltage/frequency regulation, and an upper layer solves a model predictive control (MPC) problem for day-ahead and real-time economic dispatch, incorporating carbon pricing and grid service revenues. Stability constraints derived from the impedance models will be integrated into the MPC.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated control and optimization framework for hybrid energy storage that quantifies the stability-economic trade-offs. It will provide specific guidelines for technology sizing and dispatch rules in microgrids, contributing directly to the design of low-carbon, resilient power systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight coupling of dynamic, impedance-based stability analysis (informed by Guerrero's recent modular modeling) with multi-timescale economic optimization, creating a co-design framework rather than treating stability and economics as sequential problems.

proposals.title: A Safe Deep Reinforcement Learning Framework for Stochastic Grid Scheduling with Online Robust Safety Projection
proposals.motivation: The integration of high-penetration, stochastic wind power challenges the real-time scheduling of power systems, demanding adaptive decision-making under uncertainty. While deep reinforcement learning (DRL) offers a promising adaptive framework, its direct application is hindered by the risk of proposing actions that violate critical operational constraints, such as voltage stability and line limits, compromising grid reliability. A method is needed to harness DRL's learning capability while providing rigorous, real-time safety guarantees.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid architecture combining a DRL policy with a parallel, computationally efficient online safety-verification and projection module will enable cost-effective, adaptive scheduling while providing formal, real-time guarantees against constraint violations under wind uncertainty.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage architecture. First, a deep deterministic policy gradient (DDPG) agent, trained in a simulated environment with historical wind and load data, proposes scheduling actions. Second, a parallel module, inspired by predictive safety filters and robust optimization techniques, performs online verification. This module will use scenario-based or affine adjustable robust optimization formulated for fast online evaluation. If an action is deemed unsafe across a defined uncertainty set, it is minimally projected onto the safe action set via a quadratic program, ensuring feasibility for real-time thermal, voltage, and frequency constraints before dispatch.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, provably safe DRL scheduler for wind-integrated grids, directly addressing reliability concerns. It will advance the field of safe learning-based control in power systems by providing a concrete, modular architecture that decouples performance learning from safety assurance, enabling the use of black-box RL policies in critical infrastructure.
proposals.novelty: The integration of online robust optimization as a safety filter for a continuous-action DRL agent in power scheduling is novel, moving beyond offline shielding or hard-coded safety layers. This allows the RL agent to explore and adapt while being dynamically corrected, blending adaptive learning with formal robust control guarantees.

proposals.title: A Centralized-Training-Decentralized-Execution Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning Framework for Scalable Stochastic Scheduling in Wind-Integrated Power Systems
proposals.motivation: The high penetration of stochastic wind generation creates significant challenges for power system scheduling, requiring scalable optimization methods that can handle uncertainty and distributed decision-making. Traditional centralized optimization struggles with computational complexity and privacy concerns, while purely decentralized methods may fail to achieve global optimality. This research addresses the need for a robust, scalable coordination framework for distributed energy resources (DERs) and wind farms in modern grids.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) framework, adapted from traffic flow control and employing a centralized training, decentralized execution (CTDE) paradigm, can effectively coordinate wind farm and DER aggregator agents to minimize total system operating cost and penalties under uncertainty, outperforming traditional stochastic optimization in scalability and robustness.
proposals.method: We will model wind farms and DER aggregators as cooperative agents in a partially observable Markov decision process. The framework will use a centralized critic (e.g., a value decomposition network or QMIX) trained offline on a high-fidelity power system simulator with full observability to learn a joint action-value function. Each agent will have a decentralized actor network (e.g., a deep recurrent policy network) that, during online execution, makes scheduling decisions based only on local observations (e.g., local wind forecasts, nodal prices). The global reward will combine operating cost, carbon pricing penalties, and grid stability metrics like voltage deviation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, scalable MARL architecture specifically designed for power system scheduling, demonstrating the transfer of CTDE principles from transportation to energy systems. It will provide a benchmark for decentralized, data-driven coordination of stochastic resources and offer insights into the trade-offs between communication overhead, computational scalability, and solution quality in future grid operations.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct adaptation of the CTDE-MARL paradigm, proven in traffic control, to the distinct physical and economic constraints of power systems, creating a hybrid data-driven and model-informed framework for grid scheduling that is both collaborative and privacy-preserving for individual resource owners.

proposals.title: A Deep Reinforcement Learning Framework for Multi-Regional Stochastic Dispatch with Policy-Aware Credit Transfer under High Wind Penetration
proposals.motivation: The integration of high shares of variable wind power across interconnected regions presents a critical challenge for system stability and economic operation. Traditional deterministic multi-area scheduling models struggle to manage the compounded uncertainties from diverse regional wind forecasts and heterogeneous, evolving carbon pricing policies, leading to sub-optimal reserve allocation and increased operational costs. An adaptive, system-wide coordination strategy is urgently needed to hedge against these stochastic and policy-driven risks.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-agent deep reinforcement learning (DRL) framework, enhanced with a policy-aware credit transfer mechanism, can discover coordinated dispatch strategies that significantly reduce total cost and emissions compared to isolated regional optimization or traditional stochastic programming, by explicitly learning to exploit spatial and temporal correlations in wind generation and carbon cost uncertainties.
proposals.method: We will formulate the multi-regional grid as a partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP). A centralized training with decentralized execution (CTDE) architecture will be employed, using a deep Q-network (DQN) or proximal policy optimization (PPO) as the core algorithm. The key innovation is a policy-aware credit transfer module that dynamically re-allocates learning signals among regional agents based on the similarity of their local policy gradients and the prevailing carbon/regulatory regime, facilitating efficient knowledge sharing. The environment will be simulated using a modified IEEE multi-area test system with realistic wind data and carbon price models.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel DRL framework for multi-timescale, multi-regional stochastic dispatch that explicitly internalizes policy heterogeneity. It will provide quantifiable evidence of the cost and emission benefits of adaptive, learning-based coordination over conventional methods. The proposed policy-aware credit transfer mechanism is a new contribution to multi-agent reinforcement learning for power systems.
proposals.novelty: The integration of policy-regime awareness into the credit assignment mechanism of a multi-agent DRL system for power grids is novel, moving beyond static or purely performance-based transfer. This approach directly addresses the core challenge of coordinating regions with differing regulatory and economic drivers under uncertainty.

proposals.title: Deep Reinforcement Learning for Resilient Scheduling and Voltage Stability in Wind-Integrated Microgrids
proposals.motivation: The high penetration of stochastic wind power challenges the real-time scheduling and voltage stability of modern power systems, especially in microgrids and integrated energy networks. Existing deterministic and stochastic optimization methods often struggle with computational complexity and adapting to real-time, high-dimensional uncertainties. This research addresses the critical need for an adaptive, data-driven control framework to ensure economic and stable operation under uncertainty.
proposals.hypothesis: A deep reinforcement learning (DRL) agent, trained on a co-simulation of power and communication network dynamics, can learn an optimal scheduling policy that minimizes operational cost while proactively maintaining AC bus voltage stability in a wind-integrated system with carbon pricing mechanisms.
proposals.method: We will develop a Markov Decision Process (MDP) model of a wind-integrated microgrid, incorporating carbon capture, energy storage, and communication-induced latencies. A Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) or Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) algorithm will be employed to train a scheduling agent. The agent's performance will be validated using a high-fidelity simulation environment built on modular impedance models (inspired by MEA research) to assess dynamic voltage stability, with metrics compared against model predictive control (MPC) and stochastic optimization baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel DRL-based co-optimization framework for cost and stability in renewable-dense grids. It will provide new insights into the coupling between communication network performance (latency) and power system dynamic stability, offering a scalable solution for real-time scheduling that surpasses traditional optimization methods in adaptability.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of DRL for joint economic-stability optimization, explicitly linking concepts from Guerrero's prior work on carbon-aware scheduling, voltage stability analysis in cascaded systems, and latency-aware cyber-physical modeling into a single, adaptive learning agent.

proposals.title: A Multi-Agent Hierarchical Control Framework for Dynamic Carbon Capture Networks Using Spatio-Temporal Renewable Energy Forecasts
proposals.motivation: The high energy penalty of Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS) threatens its economic viability and net carbon benefit. Simultaneously, power grids with high renewable penetration face increasing curtailment and stability challenges due to generation variability. There is a critical need to co-optimize CCS operation with grid dynamics to reduce both the carbon intensity of CCS and support grid decarbonization.
proposals.hypothesis: A decentralized multi-agent control system, where each CCS facility agent uses high-resolution spatio-temporal renewable forecasts, can dynamically schedule energy-intensive processes to act as a large-scale flexible load, thereby significantly reducing the net carbon footprint of the CCS network and providing ancillary services to enhance grid stability.
proposals.method: The research will develop a hierarchical multi-agent system (MAS) architecture. Each CCS facility is modeled as an autonomous agent using modular impedance-based models for stability analysis, similar to Guerrero's work on cascaded systems. Agents will employ model predictive control (MPC) with inputs from high-resolution spatio-temporal renewable generation forecasts and grid signals (e.g., LMP, frequency). A consensus-based algorithm, informed by information latency models from power-communication networks, will coordinate agents to collectively provide grid services while meeting capture targets.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel control framework that transforms CCS networks into active grid participants. It will provide a validated method for reducing renewable curtailment and CCS operational emissions, and contribute new stability analysis for power systems with large, forecast-responsive industrial loads.
proposals.novelty: The deep integration of spatio-temporal renewable forecasts into the real-time decision logic of distributed CCS agents, creating a synergistic load-network for grid balancing, goes beyond traditional demand response or static carbon-aware scheduling.

proposals.title: A Dynamic Niche Optimization Framework for Resilient Microgrid Operation Using Ecological Competition Models
proposals.motivation: Isolated microgrids with high renewable penetration face significant stability challenges due to resource variability and limited inertia. Traditional optimization often focuses on static economic dispatch, lacking frameworks to model dynamic, competitive interactions between generation, storage, and loads as a cohesive system for long-term resilience. Drawing an analogy to constrained ecosystems offers a novel perspective on achieving autonomous, stable balance.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling microgrid components as interacting species within an energy ecosystem, dynamic resource allocation algorithms derived from ecological niche theory and Lotka-Volterra competition models can identify operational regimes that enhance system stability and resilience beyond conventional optimization.
proposals.method: We will develop a modular impedance-based model of the microgrid, treating wind generation, battery storage, and load clusters as species with defined 'energy niches'. A modified Lotka-Volterra framework will encode their competitive and symbiotic interactions, where state variables represent stored energy and power flows. Stability will be analyzed via eigenvalue decomposition of the system's Jacobian matrix. A model predictive control (MPC) algorithm, using the ecological model as its predictive core, will schedule storage dispatch to drive the system towards its most stable equilibrium, validated through real-time hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) simulation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel bio-inspired optimization framework for microgrid stability, explicitly linking dynamic resource competition to voltage and frequency resilience metrics. It will provide a new set of stability analysis tools based on ecological principles and a practical MPC-based scheduling algorithm for isolated renewable microgrids.
proposals.novelty: The direct translation of ecological niche theory and population competition models into a formal power system control and optimization problem is unprecedented. It moves beyond simple bio-inspired algorithms (e.g., genetic algorithms) to embed ecological system dynamics directly into the microgrid's state-space representation and control logic.

proposals.title: A Cyber-Physical Co-Optimization Framework for Stability and Carbon Efficiency in Isolated Wind-Storage Microgrids
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of isolated microgrids with high renewable penetration introduces critical stability challenges due to low inertia and variable generation. Pre-2026 research often treats power and communication systems separately, yet the performance of control and protection schemes depends on data latency and network reliability. There is a pressing need for integrated models that co-optimize physical energy flows and cyber-information flows to ensure stable, low-carbon operation at the community scale.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a unified cyber-physical modeling framework, which integrates modular impedance-based stability analysis with latency-aware communication constraints, will enable the discovery of optimal, real-time dispatch strategies that simultaneously enhance AC bus voltage stability and minimize the carbon footprint of an isolated wind-storage microgrid.
proposals.method: We will adapt the modular impedance modeling technique from MEA systems to characterize the dynamic interactions between wind turbines, battery storage, and power-electronic interfaces in a microgrid. This model will be integrated with a carbon-aware optimal power flow (OPF) algorithm, informed by a carbon pricing mechanism. Crucially, the maximum latency calculation method for data centers will be scaled down to model the microgrid's local area network (LAN), embedding communication delay constraints directly into the OPF to ensure control stability under varying data traffic.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, scalable co-simulation tool for cyber-physical microgrid design. It will provide specific control and dispatch protocols that explicitly trade off carbon emissions against stability margins, a key gap in community-level planning. The results will advance the field of resilient, low-carbon microgrids by quantifying the impact of information system performance on power system stability and optimization.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct coupling of a communication network latency model with a modular impedance stability model within a carbon-constrained economic dispatch, creating a holistic cyber-physical optimization tool specifically for small-scale, isolated systems—a significant down-scaling and integration of methods previously applied to larger or separate domains.

proposals.title: A Cyber-Physical Co-Optimization Framework for Multi-Regional Power Systems with Heterogeneous Wind Resources and Carbon Policies
proposals.motivation: The global push for decarbonization is driving the integration of variable renewable energy (VRE) like wind power across interconnected grids. However, regions possess heterogeneous wind resources and are increasingly governed by distinct carbon pricing or regulatory policies (e.g., carbon taxes, cap-and-trade). This creates a complex, multi-objective optimization challenge for system operators, where traditional single-region scheduling fails to capture the full potential of inter-regional coordination for cost and carbon reduction while maintaining stability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a cyber-physical co-optimization framework, which explicitly models the interdependencies between power flow constraints, communication-induced latencies, and heterogeneous regional carbon policies, can achieve significantly lower total system cost and carbon emissions compared to decoupled or policy-agnostic scheduling approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage stochastic optimization model. The first stage performs day-ahead unit commitment and inter-regional power exchange scheduling. The second stage incorporates real-time wind power scenarios using historical data. Crucially, we will integrate an electrical circuit analogy-based latency model (inspired by Guerrero's prior work on data centers) to constrain and optimize the communication-dependent control actions across regions. The model will explicitly include region-specific carbon price inputs and energy storage dynamics, solved using a modified Benders decomposition to handle the multi-regional, stochastic structure efficiently.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantifiable methodology for joint economic and low-carbon dispatch in multi-regional systems, accounting for cyber-physical coupling. It will deliver a benchmark for evaluating how differing carbon policies impact optimal power exchange and storage utilization, offering actionable insights for policymakers and system operators.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit co-modeling of communication network performance (maximum latency) as a constraint within a multi-regional power-economic-carbon optimization, moving beyond traditional power-only or single-policy models to a truly heterogeneous, cyber-physical system perspective.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Heterogeneous Carbon Price Coordination for Multi-Regional Power Systems to Achieve Global Emission Targets
proposals.motivation: Achieving ambitious, system-wide carbon emission targets in interconnected power grids requires coordinated policy across regions with diverse generation mixes and renewable endowments. Pre-2026, carbon pricing is often applied uniformly or set arbitrarily, failing to exploit the cost-saving potential of coordinated generation, storage, and transmission dispatch across jurisdictions. An inverse design framework is needed to back-calculate the precise, region-specific carbon price trajectories that induce optimal, target-compliant system operation.
proposals.hypothesis: A system-wide emission target can be met at minimum total cost by solving an inverse optimization problem that determines heterogeneous regional carbon prices, which, when internalized by regional actors, will induce a generation and transmission dispatch identical to the cost-optimal solution for the entire interconnected system.
proposals.method: We will formulate a bi-level optimization model. The upper level minimizes total system cost (generation, storage, transmission) subject to a global emission cap, yielding the optimal dispatch. The lower level consists of regional market-clearing problems, each with a region-specific carbon price as a variable. Using Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) conditions and strong duality, we will embed the lower-level problems as constraints in a single-level Mathematical Program with Equilibrium Constraints (MPEC). This MPEC will be solved using a combination of linearization techniques and off-the-shelf solvers (e.g., Gurobi, CPLEX) to simultaneously obtain the optimal dispatch and the requisite heterogeneous carbon prices.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous computational tool for policymakers to design cost-effective, coordinated carbon pricing regimes for interconnected grids. It will quantify the economic value of policy coordination and transmission utilization in achieving climate goals. The framework will also reveal the implicit carbon price signals needed to align regional self-interest with global system objectives.
proposals.novelty: While existing work optimizes dispatch under given carbon prices, this research inverts the problem to design the carbon prices themselves. It uniquely integrates inverse optimization with power system and carbon market modeling to derive heterogeneous, spatially-differentiated price signals that guarantee a predefined emission outcome at minimum cost.

proposals.title: Minimum Energy Storage Capacity Determination for Grid Stability Under a Fixed Electricity Cost Constraint: A Dual Optimization Framework
proposals.motivation: The integration of high-penetration renewables and distributed energy resources challenges grid stability, often requiring significant energy storage investments. Current optimization models typically minimize total system cost, including storage, but do not explicitly answer the critical planning question: given a fixed budget for electricity procurement and generation, what is the absolute minimum storage capacity required to ensure stability? This dual formulation is essential for cost-constrained grid operators and microgrid designers in the pre-2026 era of volatile component pricing.
proposals.hypothesis: For a given fixed electricity cost envelope, there exists a non-trivial, quantifiable minimum energy storage capacity that ensures dynamic stability (voltage/frequency) in a renewable-integrated power system, and this minimum can be found by reformulating traditional cost-minimization problems into a stability-constrained, storage-minimization dual.
proposals.method: The research will employ a modular impedance modeling approach, extending Guerrero's prior work on cascaded systems, to formulate stability constraints for a grid with wind generation and storage. A bi-level optimization framework will be developed: the outer loop minimizes storage capacity (energy and power ratings) using a genetic algorithm, while the inner loop, solved via linear programming, dispatches resources to meet load and stability metrics under the fixed electricity cost constraint. The 'fixed cost' will integrate pre-2026 carbon pricing mechanisms and generation costs, with stability validated through eigenvalue analysis of the derived system model.
