proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel optimization tool for system planners to determine storage requirements under hard budgetary limits. It will contribute a formalized 'dual problem' perspective to the energy management literature, linking economic constraints directly to physical stability needs. The results will offer practical benchmarks for storage sizing in microgrids and active distribution networks.
proposals.novelty: While cost-minimization with storage is standard, inverting the problem to minimize storage for a given cost—explicitly treating cost as a constraint and stability as a non-negotiable objective—is a novel and non-trivial dual formulation. The integration of modular impedance-based stability constraints directly into this dual optimization framework is also a significant methodological advance beyond typical steady-state power balance models.

proposals.title: Stochastic Reliability-Aware Control for Multi-Energy Microgrids: Integrating Component Degradation and Failure Models
proposals.motivation: Modern multi-energy systems (MES) integrate power, thermal, and communication networks to enhance efficiency and resilience. However, existing control and optimization models often assume perfect component reliability, which is unrealistic and can lead to severe performance degradation or cascading failures when stochastic degradation and faults occur. Addressing this gap is critical for the practical deployment and robust operation of MES, especially in safety-critical applications like more-electric aircraft and islanded microgrids.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating stochastic models of component degradation and failure into the control and stability analysis of multi-energy systems will reveal critical vulnerabilities and enable the design of control strategies that significantly improve system resilience and lifetime performance compared to deterministic, reliability-ignorant approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a modular, multi-timescale modeling framework. Component degradation (e.g., in power converters, batteries, communication links) will be modeled using stochastic processes like Markov chains or Wiener processes, informed by reliability data. These models will be integrated into a holistic MES model using impedance/modular methods, extending Guerrero's prior work on cascaded systems. A stochastic model predictive control (SMPC) architecture will be designed to optimize energy dispatch and voltage regulation while explicitly accounting for the probabilistic health state and failure risk of components.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel modeling and control framework that quantifies the impact of component reliability on MES stability and performance. It will deliver actionable control policies that proactively manage degradation, extending asset life and preventing catastrophic failures, thereby advancing the field from deterministic optimization to reliability-aware system design.
proposals.novelty: While component reliability is studied in isolation, its integration into the dynamic, coupled control of multi-energy systems is novel. This proposal uniquely combines stochastic degradation models with modular impedance-based stability analysis and SMPC, a synthesis not yet explored in Guerrero's prior work on MES control and stability.

proposals.title: A Modular Impedance-Based Stability and Power Management Framework for Ship-to-Grid Integration in Smart Port Microgrids
proposals.motivation: The decarbonization of maritime transport is driving the electrification of ships and port infrastructure, creating complex, interconnected microgrids. Pre-2026, the dynamic interaction between a ship's onboard power system (a cascaded microgrid) and the port's smart energy supply, including high-power charging and renewable generation, poses significant stability and power quality challenges that are not well understood. This research addresses the critical need for a unified modeling and control framework to ensure reliable, low-carbon energy exchange.
proposals.hypothesis: The stability and power quality of the integrated ship-port microgrid system can be guaranteed, and its operational performance optimized, by developing a modular impedance-based interaction model and a hierarchical, information-aware control strategy that coordinates the shipboard and port-side energy management systems.
proposals.method: We will extend Guerrero's modular impedance modeling approach for cascaded systems (from aircraft) to model the ship microgrid and port infrastructure as interconnected subsystems. A dual-layer control architecture will be proposed: a local primary control layer using droop-based methods for voltage/frequency regulation, and a centralized secondary layer (port energy management system) that uses real-time power and information flow data to solve a low-carbon optimization problem. This optimization will coordinate ship charging/discharging, port energy storage, and local renewables, using methods akin to carbon-aware scheduling in power systems, to minimize operational cost and emissions while respecting stability constraints derived from the impedance model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, generalized stability analysis tool for ship-to-grid integration and a co-simulation testbed combining power and communication network latencies. It will deliver a practical control framework that enables ports to act as proactive grid participants, facilitating high penetration of shore power and renewables while maintaining system stability.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of modular impedance stability analysis for cascaded power-electronic systems (traditionally for aircraft or data centers) with the specific, high-power, bidirectional energy flow context of smart ports, and the integration of this physical model into a carbon-aware, communication-dependent energy management optimization loop.

proposals.title: Transferable Graph Neural Networks for Real-Time Stability Assessment in Shipboard Power Distribution Networks
proposals.motivation: Shipboard Power Distribution Networks (SPDNs) are complex, reconfigurable microgrids with stringent stability requirements. Traditional model-based analysis struggles with real-time assessment under dynamic operational states and fault conditions. A data-driven approach is needed to capture complex power flow and stability constraints for enhanced resilience and power quality in naval and commercial marine vessels.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Graph Neural Network (GNN) pre-trained on high-fidelity simulation data from a canonical SPDN topology can be effectively transferred, via transfer learning, to accurately predict voltage stability and power flow in novel or reconfigured SPDN architectures, outperforming conventional, topology-specific models.
proposals.method: We will develop a modular GNN architecture based on pre-2026 Message Passing Neural Networks (MPNNs) to model the SPDN as a graph with buses as nodes and lines as edges, with node/edge features encoding electrical parameters and measurements. The model will be pre-trained on a comprehensive dataset generated from a high-fidelity, modular impedance-based simulation model (extending Guerrero's prior aircraft work to marine systems). We will then implement a transfer learning framework, fine-tuning the pre-trained GNN on limited data from target SPDN variants to assess its generalization to different shipboard configurations.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, transferable GNN framework for real-time SPDN analysis, reducing the need for extensive retraining for each new design. It will provide a robust tool for predicting voltage stability violations and optimizing reconfiguration strategies, directly enhancing the operational resilience of marine microgrids.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of GNNs with transfer learning specifically for the SPDN domain, moving beyond single-topology models. This addresses the core challenge of system reconfiguration and variability in marine grids, a step beyond most pre-2026 GNN applications in terrestrial power systems.

proposals.title: A Bi-Level Optimization Framework for Shipboard Power System Sizing Under Unified Reliability Network and Economic Constraints
proposals.motivation: The electrification of shipboard systems, including propulsion and pulsed loads, demands optimal sizing of energy assets (e.g., generators, batteries, fuel cells) to ensure operational feasibility and cost-effectiveness. Pre-2026, the Unified Reliability Network (URN) framework has been proposed to model the interdependencies between power and communication networks, but its application as a hard constraint for capacity planning in maritime microgrids remains unexplored. Addressing this gap is critical for designing resilient, all-electric ships that meet stringent performance and survivability requirements.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that formulating the optimal sizing problem as a dual optimization—minimizing capital and operational expenditure while strictly adhering to URN-derived latency and reliability constraints—will yield a Pareto-optimal frontier of asset configurations that are both economically viable and robust against cascading failures.
proposals.method: The method integrates modular impedance modeling (from MEA research) for AC bus stability analysis of the ship's power system with an electrical circuit analogy (from data center work) to translate URN communication latency into equivalent power network constraints. A bi-level optimization architecture will be employed: the outer loop uses a genetic algorithm for asset sizing, while the inner loop solves a low-carbon economic dispatch (extending prior carbon pricing work) to evaluate operational costs and dynamic stability under stochastic load profiles. Constraint handling will explicitly incorporate URN-based maximum latency bounds.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, co-design optimization tool for naval architects and power engineers, explicitly bridging reliability theory and economic planning. It will provide quantifiable trade-off curves between system cost, carbon footprint, and URN-guaranteed reliability for shipboard microgrids, advancing the design of integrated energy systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in treating the URN not merely as an analysis tool but as a set of primal constraints for a dual economic-sizing problem, a synthesis not previously attempted in maritime microgrid literature. Furthermore, the integration of circuit-analogy-based latency constraints with modular stability models creates a unique cross-domain optimization framework.

proposals.title: Nonlinear Model Predictive Control for Hybrid Energy Storage Systems with Explicit Degradation and Operational Constraints in Microgrids
proposals.motivation: The integration of battery (BESS) and battery-thermal (BTESS) energy storage is critical for microgrid stability and renewable energy utilization, but existing control designs often use simplified linear models. This neglects key nonlinear dynamics, hard power/energy limits, and degradation costs, leading to suboptimal performance, reduced lifespan, and potential system instability under stress. A control framework that explicitly incorporates these realistic constraints is urgently needed for reliable and economical microgrid operation.
proposals.hypothesis: A nonlinear model predictive control (NMPC) strategy that explicitly incorporates detailed nonlinear HESS dynamics, hard operational constraints, and a convexified degradation cost model will significantly improve microgrid voltage/frequency stability and total operational economy compared to linear or rule-based controllers.
proposals.method: We will develop high-fidelity nonlinear models for BESS (including state-of-charge, temperature, and C-rate effects) and BTESS (coupling electrical and thermal energy states). These will be integrated into a modular NMPC architecture, similar to Guerrero's prior impedance modeling work. The optimization will solve a multi-objective cost function (tracking, degradation, fuel cost) using a real-time iteration scheme, with hard constraints on power/energy limits and internal states. The carbon-aware optimization framework from the researcher's recent work will be extended to include the degradation trade-offs.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, practically implementable NMPC design tool for HESS that guarantees safe operation within physical limits while optimizing for cost and longevity. It will provide a rigorous methodology to quantify the stability and economic benefits of detailed nonlinear modeling in storage control, advancing the co-design of power and communication networks for resilient microgrids.
proposals.novelty: The integration of a convex, cycle-based degradation cost model for both BESS and BTESS within a real-time NMPC framework, explicitly trading off short-term grid performance against long-term asset health, moves beyond typical linear quadratic or heuristic approaches in microgrid control.

proposals.title: Model Predictive Control for Integrated Frequency Regulation and Economic Dispatch in Grids with Hybrid Energy Storage Systems
proposals.motivation: The increasing penetration of intermittent renewable energy sources intensifies the need for fast-responding resources to maintain grid frequency stability while minimizing operational costs. Hybrid Energy Storage Systems (HESS), combining technologies like batteries and supercapacitors, offer complementary power and energy characteristics ideal for this dual task. However, existing dispatch strategies often treat frequency regulation and economic energy scheduling separately, leading to suboptimal performance and accelerated HESS degradation.
proposals.hypothesis: A unified Model Predictive Control (MPC) framework can optimally coordinate the fast power response of a HESS for frequency regulation with its slower energy dispatch for economic objectives, thereby improving overall system stability and reducing operational costs compared to decoupled or rule-based strategies.
proposals.method: The method involves developing a multi-timescale MPC architecture. A high-level economic MPC, using a receding horizon with forecasts for load and renewable generation, will solve a modified optimal power flow to determine base-point schedules and HESS energy targets over a 15-minute to 1-hour horizon. A low-level regulatory MPC, operating on a second-to-minute timescale, will use a linearized grid frequency model to optimally allocate real-time frequency regulation power signals between the battery (for sustained energy) and supercapacitor (for high-power transients) components of the HESS, respecting constraints from the high-level controller. The control problem will be formulated as a quadratic program, solved at each time step using established solvers like OSQP.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, unified control framework that explicitly bridges the timescales of economic dispatch and fast frequency regulation for HESS. It will provide a quantitative method to trade-off regulation performance against storage degradation and energy cost. The results will offer practical control architectures for enhancing the stability and economic viability of renewable-dominant microgrids and bulk power systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight, constraint-aware coupling of frequency stability control (a dynamic grid performance metric) with economic dispatch within a single MPC optimization, specifically tailored for the heterogeneous dynamics of HESS, moving beyond their typical use for single objectives like power smoothing or arbitrage.

proposals.title: A Fleet-Level Digital Twin Framework for Battery Health Analytics in Grid-Scale Energy Storage Systems
proposals.motivation: The rapid deployment of grid-scale battery energy storage systems (BESS) and electric vehicle (EV) fleets creates a critical need for scalable health monitoring to ensure system stability and economic viability. Existing methods focus on single-battery diagnostics, lacking the ability to aggregate and analyze health data across thousands of heterogeneous units to predict fleet-wide performance degradation and optimize logistics. This gap hinders reliable power dispatch, secondary frequency control, and asset management in modern power systems.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical, modular digital twin framework, integrating impedance-based state estimation and data-driven prognostics, can accurately predict fleet-level state-of-health (SOH) trends and failure rates by fusing heterogeneous operational data from distributed battery units.
proposals.method: We propose a two-layer architecture. First, a local layer uses modular impedance modeling, inspired by Guerrero's aircraft power system work, to estimate SOH and internal parameters from operational voltage/current data for each battery unit. Second, a fleet-level aggregator employs a graph neural network (GNN) to model the interdependencies and shared stressor profiles across the fleet, fed with latency-aware communication constraints. Fleet health is predicted via a Bayesian aggregation of individual prognostics, enabling logistics optimization for maintenance and replacement.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable analytics tool for grid operators and fleet managers to forecast aggregate battery capacity and failure modes, directly supporting grid stability and low-carbon optimization. It will advance the field by creating a formal link between individual battery electrochemistry, communication network performance, and system-level power control strategies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of physics-based modular impedance models with fleet-level graph-based learning for health analytics, explicitly incorporating power-communication network latencies—a direct extension of Guerrero's recent interdisciplinary research—into a unified BESS management framework.

proposals.title: A Soft-Constrained Economic Dispatch Framework for Battery Energy Storage Fleets Using Quadratic Imbalance Cost Integration
proposals.motivation: The optimal dispatch of distributed battery energy storage systems (BESS) is critical for grid stability and renewable energy integration. Traditional control methods enforce strict State of Charge (SoC) balancing as a hard constraint, which can force uneconomical or damaging charge/discharge cycles during extreme price volatility or grid contingencies. A more flexible approach is needed to allow rational, cost-aware trade-offs between fleet balance and financial/operational objectives.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a quadratic cost of SoC imbalance directly into the economic objective function, rather than enforcing balance as a hard constraint, will yield a more cost-effective and resilient fleet dispatch strategy that automatically adapts to market and grid conditions.
proposals.method: We will formulate a model predictive control (MPC) optimization problem where the objective function minimizes the sum of real energy transaction costs and a virtual quadratic cost term penalizing the variance of SoC across the fleet. The penalty coefficient will be a tunable parameter linking imbalance to economic value. The problem will be solved using quadratic programming (QP) solvers. Performance will be validated via simulation in a modular impedance-based framework, analyzing impacts on AC bus voltage stability and comparing financial and balance metrics against hard-constrained benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel control paradigm for BESS fleets that explicitly quantifies the economic value of SoC balance. It will deliver a tunable framework for system operators to manage the trade-off between grid-support service quality (via balance) and operational cost, enhancing the business case for distributed storage.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the treatment of SoC balance not as a constraint but as a monetizable commodity with a defined cost function within the optimization, allowing the solver to dynamically 'purchase' imbalance when economically rational, a departure from all prior hard-constrained methods.

proposals.title: A Lexicographic Optimization Framework for Constraint-First Battery Energy Storage Management in AC Microgrids
proposals.motivation: In AC microgrids and islanded power systems, maintaining state-of-charge (SoC) balance across distributed battery energy storage systems (BESS) is critical for system stability, lifespan, and reliable power sharing. Existing multi-objective optimization methods, such as weighted-sum or Pareto-based approaches, inherently trade off SoC balance against economic objectives like operational cost, risking battery health under certain conditions. A method that guarantees SoC balance as a hard constraint is needed for high-reliability applications.
proposals.hypothesis: By structuring the BESS dispatch problem using lexicographic ordering from operations research—where perfect SoC equalization is solved as a primary, absolute constraint before cost minimization—the proposed framework will guarantee battery health and system stability without the performance trade-offs of conventional multi-objective methods.
proposals.method: The method formulates a two-stage, lexicographic optimization problem. Stage 1 is a constrained optimization whose sole objective is to achieve perfect SoC balance (or minimize balance error to zero) across all BESS units, defining a feasible operational envelope. Stage 2 performs economic dispatch (minimizing generation and degradation costs) but is constrained to the solution set from Stage 1. This will be implemented in a hierarchical control architecture, with the primary controller solving the lexicographic problem using a mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) solver and sending setpoints to local BESS controllers. Stability analysis will be conducted using modular impedance models to verify the impact of the new dispatch strategy on small-signal stability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel control paradigm that absolutely prioritizes battery health and grid stability over economics, crucial for critical infrastructure. It will deliver a formal, implementable framework for lexicographic optimization in power systems, extending its use from purely operational research to real-time energy management. The resulting control strategy is expected to enhance BESS lifespan and system reliability in microgrids.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the strict inversion of priority using lexicographic ordering, transforming SoC balance from a soft, trade-off objective into a non-negotiable system constraint. This approach is distinct from all prior weighted or Pareto-optimal BESS management strategies documented in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Graph-Theoretic Energy Management System for Microgrids with Dynamic Topology Reconfiguration
proposals.motivation: Modern microgrids (MGs) increasingly incorporate mobile or plug-and-play Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) and distributed generators, leading to a dynamic network topology. Existing Energy Management Systems (EMS) predominantly assume a static architecture, which limits operational flexibility, resilience, and the efficient integration of mobile resources. This gap becomes critical for applications like emergency power systems, naval shipboard power, and evolving community MGs, where connection points can change in real-time.
proposals.hypothesis: An EMS that explicitly models the MG as a dynamic graph, where nodes (BESS, converters, loads) and edges (connection status) can change, will achieve superior stability and economic performance compared to static-topology EMS under the same conditions of resource mobility and demand fluctuation.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-layer hierarchical control architecture. The primary layer uses a dynamic graph model, updated via a distributed communication protocol (inspired by the PI consensus-based methods from Guerrero's prior work), to track topology changes. The secondary, optimization layer will employ a receding-horizon Model Predictive Control (MPC) framework. The MPC's constraints and cost function will be dynamically reconfigured based on the real-time graph, optimizing power dispatch and voltage regulation. Stability analysis will utilize modular impedance-based methods, extending Guerrero's aircraft power system work to ground-based MGs with variable interconnection.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal framework and control algorithms for EMS in dynamically reconfiguring MGs, directly enhancing operational resilience and asset utilization. It will deliver a novel stability analysis method for such systems and validate the approach through hardware-in-the-loop simulation of a lab-scale MG with plug-and-play BESS units.
proposals.novelty: While prior work addresses MG reconfiguration for fault isolation, this proposal uniquely focuses on continuous, intentional topology changes for optimal EMS, integrating dynamic graph theory with real-time MPC. It moves beyond static network assumptions in EMS design, a significant departure from standard practice pre-2026.

proposals.title: An Ecological Succession-Inspired Energy Management System for Heterogeneous Battery Energy Storage Fleets
proposals.motivation: The integration of heterogeneous battery energy storage system (BESS) fleets, comprising both new and second-life units with varying states-of-health (SoH), presents a critical challenge for grid stability and economic operation. Existing energy management systems (EMS) often treat such fleets homogeneously or with simple prioritization, failing to optimize the aggregate lifetime and performance. This inefficiency hinders the economic viability of second-life batteries and the reliable provision of grid services like frequency regulation and peak shaving.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling a heterogeneous BESS fleet as an energy ecosystem and applying principles of ecological succession and niche partitioning, an adaptive, role-based EMS can dynamically optimize state-of-charge (SoC) balancing and task assignment, thereby extending the aggregate useful life and improving the overall performance-cost ratio of the fleet.
proposals.method: The method involves developing a hierarchical control architecture. First, a modular impedance-based model, inspired by the researcher's prior work in cascaded systems, will represent each BESS unit's dynamic characteristics. Second, an ecological analogy engine will classify units into 'successional roles' (e.g., pioneer, climax) based on real-time SoH, capacity, and internal resistance. Third, a distributed optimization layer, using a consensus-based algorithm with information on latency and carbon pricing, will adaptively assign grid service tasks (e.g., fast response vs. long-duration storage) to suitable roles, ensuring balanced degradation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel EMS framework that explicitly maximizes the economic lifetime of heterogeneous BESS fleets. It will provide a validated control strategy for leveraging second-life batteries effectively, enhancing grid stability services. The research will also formalize a cross-disciplinary methodology for applying ecological principles to engineered power systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct analogical transfer of ecological succession and niche theory to BESS fleet management, moving beyond traditional optimization to a role-based, adaptive paradigm. This contrasts with existing SoC balancing methods that do not dynamically reconfigure operational roles based on a unit's degradation trajectory within the fleet ecosystem.

proposals.title: A Dual-Layer Adaptive Energy Management System with Joint Parameter-State Estimation for Robust SoC Equalization in Heterogeneous Battery Packs
proposals.motivation: The increasing deployment of battery energy storage systems (BESS) in microgrids and renewable integration faces a critical challenge in managing cell-to-cell heterogeneity, which accelerates due to aging and dynamic operating conditions. Existing energy management systems (EMS) often rely on fixed parameters and simplified models, leading to suboptimal state-of-charge (SoC) equalization, reduced pack capacity, and compromised system stability over the long term. This problem is central to the reliability and longevity of modern power-electronic-dominated grids and more-electric aircraft systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a real-time, data-driven joint parameter and state estimator with a reconfigurable nonlinear controller will enable an EMS to adaptively maintain optimal SoC balancing and system stability despite significant initial parameter uncertainty and progressive cell degradation.
proposals.method: The method employs a dual-layer architecture. First, a data-driven observer, based on an adaptive extended Kalman filter (AEKF) or an unscented Kalman filter (UKF), will perform joint estimation of cell-specific parameters (e.g., internal resistance, capacity) and states (SoC, voltage). Second, this continuously updated model will feed a reconfigurable nonlinear controller, designed using sliding mode or backstepping control principles, which dynamically adjusts its balancing strategy (e.g., active cell-to-cell or cell-to-pack). The system will be validated through hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) simulation using a cascaded converter setup, analyzing bus voltage stability under the proposed EMS.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a validated, adaptive EMS framework that explicitly addresses parameter drift and heterogeneity for robust long-term operation. It will provide new insights into the co-design of estimation and control for power-electronic-based BESS, directly enhancing system stability and usable capacity. The results will offer a concrete methodology for extending battery pack lifespan in grid and transportation applications.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight, real-time coupling of joint parameter-state estimation with a reconfigurable nonlinear control law specifically for SoC equalization, moving beyond fixed-model approaches or decoupled estimation-control designs prevalent in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Online Adaptive Co-optimization of Operational Cost and Battery SoC Balancing with Real-Time Parameter Identification for Grid Stability
proposals.motivation: The integration of battery energy storage systems (BESS) is critical for grid stability and low-carbon power systems, as seen in modern microgrids and renewable integration. However, optimal BESS operation is hindered by the degradation and performance variability of individual battery cells, which leads to inefficient SoC balancing and suboptimal economic dispatch over the asset's lifetime. Existing optimization frameworks often rely on fixed, offline battery models, making them vulnerable to real-world parameter drift and reducing system reliability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating an online, adaptive parameter identification module into a co-optimization framework will enable dynamic adjustment of cost functions and balancing targets, thereby maintaining near-optimal economic performance and improved SoC balancing throughout the battery system's operational life.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-layer modular control architecture. The lower layer employs a recursive least squares (RLS) algorithm with forgetting factor for online identification of battery equivalent circuit model parameters (e.g., internal resistance, capacity) from operational voltage and current data. The upper layer formulates a model predictive control (MPC) problem that co-optimizes energy cost (considering time-varying electricity prices) and SoC balancing, using the updated parameters to refine the system constraints and objective function. Stability analysis, inspired by modular impedance methods, will ensure robust performance of the cascaded identification-optimization loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a robust, self-calibrating control framework for BESS that enhances long-term economic viability and reliability in grid applications. It will provide a formal methodology for closing the loop between real-time system identification and multi-objective optimization in power-electronic-dominated systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the closed-loop, adaptive integration of online parameter identification directly into a real-time co-optimization for cost and SoC, moving beyond static models to a lifetime-aware control strategy that responds autonomously to battery health degradation.

proposals.title: A Distributed Multi-Objective Battery Management Strategy Based on Ideal Free Distribution for Enhanced System Longevity and Stability
proposals.motivation: The increasing integration of battery energy storage systems (BESS) into microgrids and renewable energy systems demands advanced management to balance economic operation with long-term health. Existing centralized BMS often struggle to simultaneously optimize operational cost and achieve precise, real-time state-of-charge (SoC) balancing across heterogeneous cells or packs, leading to accelerated degradation and reduced system stability. A novel control paradigm is needed to address this multi-objective challenge efficiently.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling battery cells/packs as autonomous agents competing for a shared energy resource, governed by principles of Ideal Free Distribution (IFD) from theoretical ecology, will yield a distributed optimization algorithm that converges to a system-wide equilibrium. This equilibrium will inherently minimize total operational cost while actively equalizing SoC, analogous to a Nash equilibrium in ecological systems.
proposals.method: We will formulate a multi-objective optimization problem with competing cost functions for wear, efficiency, and SoC deviation. Inspired by IFD models, we will design a distributed, agent-based control architecture where each battery unit acts as an autonomous agent. Each agent will iteratively adjust its power set-point using a consensus-based algorithm (e.g., a modified distributed gradient method) to maximize its local 'fitness' (inverse of local cost) under global power constraints. The system's convergence to a stable, efficient equilibrium will be analyzed using Lyapunov stability theory and modular impedance concepts, similar to prior work on cascaded systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, bio-inspired distributed control framework for BESS that explicitly co-optimizes economic dispatch and SoC balancing. It will provide a formal stability analysis of the resulting equilibrium and demonstrate through simulation (e.g., in a wind-integrated microgrid context) improved battery pack longevity and enhanced system-level voltage stability compared to conventional hierarchical methods.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct translation of ecological resource competition models (Ideal Free Distribution) into a engineering control framework for BESS, creating a non-obvious link between theoretical ecology and power electronics system optimization to solve a persistent multi-objective problem.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Mechanism Design and Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning Framework for Incentive-Compatible BESS Coordination in Distribution Grids
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of privately-owned Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) introduces strategic agents with private cost and constraint information into grid operations. Traditional cooperative control and centralized optimization fail under these conditions, risking market manipulation, inefficient dispatch, and system instability. A framework is needed to align private incentives with system-wide goals like voltage stability and renewable integration.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid coordination framework, combining a formally incentive-compatible market mechanism with a multi-agent deep reinforcement learning (MADRL) policy layer, can achieve near-optimal, stable grid operation while guaranteeing truthful participation from strategic BESS owners under real-world uncertainties.
proposals.method: We will first design a core market mechanism based on Vickrey-Clarke-Groves (VCG) auctions or a distributed dual decomposition approach to elicit truthful bids for power/energy. This mechanism will be integrated with a MADRL framework, where each BESS agent employs a Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) or Multi-Agent Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (MADDPG) algorithm. The agents will learn adaptive policies to fulfill their market commitments despite local forecast errors and communication delays, using only local observations and limited shared information. Stability will be analyzed using modular impedance-based methods extended to the multi-agent domain.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, deployable architecture for distributed BESS coordination that is provably robust to strategic behavior. It will advance the integration of economic mechanism design with data-driven control in power systems, offering a new paradigm for stability-guaranteed, incentive-aware grid management. The resulting framework will be validated via simulation on a modified IEEE distribution test feeder.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the tight, formal integration of mechanism design for incentive compatibility with a MADRL layer for adaptive physical control, moving beyond purely cooperative or purely economic models. This addresses the simultaneous challenge of private information and dynamic uncertainty in a unified structure.

proposals.title: Robust Model Predictive Control for State-of-Charge Management in Microgrids with High Renewable Penetration
proposals.motivation: The integration of intermittent renewable generation and dynamic loads in microgrids creates significant challenges for battery energy storage system (BESS) management. Traditional control strategies often fail to optimally manage the state-of-charge (SoC) under forecast uncertainty, risking grid instability, reduced battery lifespan, and inefficient energy dispatch. A robust control framework is needed to ensure reliable operation while maximizing economic and technical performance.
proposals.hypothesis: A robust model predictive control (RMPC) framework, adapted from process industry applications, can effectively manage BESS SoC under uncertain forecasts of load and renewable generation, thereby improving microgrid voltage stability and operational economics compared to conventional predictive or rule-based controllers.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-time-scale RMPC architecture. The outer layer uses forecasts to solve a finite-horizon optimal power flow problem, minimizing a cost function of energy purchase, carbon cost, and battery degradation. The inner layer employs a tube-based MPC algorithm to robustly track the reference SoC trajectory, explicitly handling forecast errors bounded via historical data. The controller will be tested in a real-time simulation platform (e.g., OPAL-RT) with a detailed model of a modular, cascaded power-electronic system, integrating impedance-based stability analysis from the researcher's prior aircraft work.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, practically implementable control framework for microgrid BESS management that guarantees robust performance against forecast uncertainty. It will contribute a formal method for co-optimizing economic dispatch, voltage stability, and battery health, directly applicable to low-carbon power systems and data center power-communication networks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tailored application of process-industry RMPC theory to the specific problem of microgrid SoC management, explicitly integrating carbon pricing mechanisms and modular impedance stability constraints into the control formulation, which is not common in existing power-centric MPC literature.

proposals.title: Receding-Horizon Model Predictive Control for Optimal Multi-Energy Dispatch and Voltage Stability in All-Electric Ships
proposals.motivation: The transition to all-electric ships (AES) with integrated energy storage, renewables, and propulsion loads creates a complex, tightly coupled multi-energy system. Pre-2026 research highlights significant challenges in managing dynamic power flows, ensuring DC/AC bus voltage stability, and optimizing fuel-to-power efficiency under highly variable voyage conditions. Existing rule-based or static optimization methods lack the predictive capability and constraint handling needed for real-time, voyage-wide optimal performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Model Predictive Control (MPC) framework, adapted from process industries, can simultaneously optimize fuel consumption, manage state-of-charge of energy storage, and enforce bus voltage stability constraints in real-time for an AES, outperforming conventional control strategies in both economic and dynamic performance.
proposals.method: We will develop a linearized or piecewise-affine state-space model of the ship's multi-energy system, integrating diesel generators, batteries, propulsion drives, and hotel loads. A centralized MPC controller will be designed to solve a finite-horizon quadratic programming problem at each time step, minimizing a cost function of fuel use and component degradation, subject to power balance, storage dynamics, and voltage stability constraints derived from modular impedance models. The receding-horizon implementation will use forecasted load and voyage profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a validated real-time control framework for AES energy management that explicitly co-optimizes economic dispatch and system stability. It will provide a methodological bridge between process-control MPC and maritime power systems, delivering quantitative performance benchmarks against existing methods like droop control or offline optimization.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tailored application of industrial MPC to the unique, high-power, low-inertia environment of an AES, explicitly integrating modular impedance-based voltage stability analysis as dynamic constraints within the MPC's receding-horizon optimization, a synthesis not previously demonstrated.

proposals.title: Deep Reinforcement Learning for Adaptive Microgrid Voltage and Frequency Control Under High-Penetration Renewable Uncertainty
proposals.motivation: The increasing integration of intermittent renewable energy sources and dynamic loads, such as electric vehicle charging, introduces severe uncertainty into microgrid operation, challenging traditional linear control methods. This uncertainty, akin to the unpredictable environments faced by mobile robots, can lead to voltage instability and frequency deviations, compromising power quality and system reliability. Developing control policies that adapt in real-time to these 'navigation conditions' is critical for resilient, islanded, or weak-grid-connected microgrids.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model-free deep reinforcement learning (DRL) control agent, trained on a high-fidelity microgrid model with stochastic disturbances, can learn adaptive policies that outperform conventional droop and PI controllers in maintaining voltage and frequency stability under high uncertainty. The agent's performance will be robust to unseen disturbance profiles not encountered during training.
proposals.method: We will formulate the microgrid secondary control problem as a Markov Decision Process (MDP), with states including bus voltages, frequency, power mismatches, and state-of-charge of energy storage. The action space will be control signals for distributed generators and storage converters. A Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) or Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) algorithm will be employed due to their suitability for continuous action spaces. The agent will be trained in a simulation environment built in MATLAB/Simulink or OPAL-RT, incorporating stochastic models for renewable generation and load changes derived from real pre-2026 data.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a validated DRL-based control framework that autonomously adapts to uncertain microgrid operating conditions, enhancing dynamic stability. It will provide a novel control paradigm, moving from model-based to learning-based adaptive control for power systems, with demonstrated performance metrics (e.g., reduced voltage deviation, faster frequency recovery) compared to established methods.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct translation of advanced DRL techniques from robotics (designed for uncertain physical environments) to the microgrid stability problem, creating an agent that learns to 'navigate' the microgrid's state-space under disturbance. This approach moves beyond using RL for simple set-point optimization to direct, low-level dynamic control of voltage and frequency.

ideas.title: Co-optimization of Grid Storage and Generation Portfolios Under Deep Decarbonization
ideas.description: This research develops a multi-stage stochastic optimization model that co-optimizes investment in generation, transmission, and multiple energy storage technologies (e.g., Li-ion, pumped hydro, flow batteries) with their detailed hourly operation over a multi-decade horizon. The model explicitly captures the value-stacking capabilities of storage (energy arbitrage, capacity, ancillary services) and their interactions within a future grid dominated by variable renewables. By solving for the least-cost portfolio that meets reliability and emissions targets, it reveals the optimal roles, sizes, and market values of different storage types, providing a robust, systems-level analysis far beyond simple technology comparisons.

ideas.title: Emissions-First Hybrid Converter Design for Carbon-Aware Battery Management
ideas.description: This research direction inverts the typical design priority for grid-connected battery systems by first defining a strict constraint of minimizing operational carbon emissions. It then designs novel hybrid power converter topologies (combining redundancy-based, modular, and cascaded structures) specifically to enable highly granular, cell-level state-of-charge (SoC) control. This granular control is used not just for battery health, but as the primary mechanism to dynamically align battery charging/discharging with the real-time carbon intensity of the grid, treating stored energy as a carbon-aware asset. The resulting economic costs of this operation are analyzed as a secondary trade-off, creating a new paradigm for power electronic hardware that is fundamentally architected for carbon minimization rather than efficiency or cost alone.

ideas.title: Co-optimized Scheduling of Multi-Renewable Generation and Multi-Temporal Demand Flexibility
ideas.description: This research develops an integrated scheduling model that simultaneously optimizes the dispatch of wind and solar photovoltaic generation alongside the active participation of heterogeneous flexible loads. The core innovation is to leverage the distinct diurnal and seasonal patterns of wind and solar availability to inform and target specific demand-side response strategies, such as aligning solar over-generation with daytime deferrable loads and using wind power for overnight thermal pre-charging. This approach moves beyond simply adding more variables, creating a synergistic framework where the inherent variability of a multi-renewable portfolio is not just balanced but actively managed by matching it with temporally appropriate demand-side resources. The outcome is a scheduling strategy that minimizes curtailment, reduces reliance on conventional peaking plants, and enhances overall grid stability at higher renewable penetration levels.

ideas.title: Adaptive Co-optimization of Cost and SoC Balancing with Online Parameter Identification
ideas.description: This research develops a robust optimization framework that jointly minimizes operational cost and ensures state-of-charge (SoC) balancing for battery systems. The key innovation is the integration of an online, adaptive parameter identification module that continuously estimates battery health and performance characteristics from operational data. This allows the core co-optimization algorithm to dynamically adjust its cost models and balancing targets in response to real-world degradation and variability. The resulting system maintains optimal performance over the asset's lifetime without requiring perfect prior knowledge or manual recalibration.

ideas.title: Multi-Agent, Renewables-Aware Control for Dynamic Carbon Capture Networks
ideas.description: This research proposes a decentralized multi-agent system for coordinating a network of carbon capture and storage (CCS) facilities, where each facility agent dynamically schedules its energy-intensive processes. The key innovation is the deep integration of high-resolution, spatio-temporal models for variable renewable generation (solar PV and wind) into each agent's decision-making logic. This enables the CCS network to act as a large-scale, flexible load that can absorb excess renewable power, reduce curtailment, and provide grid-balancing services, thereby simultaneously lowering the net carbon footprint of the CCS operations themselves and enhancing overall grid decarbonization.

ideas.title: Multi-Objective Optimization for Battery Management Inspired by Ecosystem Resource Allocation
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to develop advanced optimization processes that jointly minimize operational cost and ensure precise SoC balancing in battery systems. By drawing an analogy from theoretical ecology—specifically models of ideal free distribution and ecosystem resource allocation—we adapt a solution where populations balance individual fitness and resource competition to achieve a stable, efficient equilibrium. The adapted idea is to create a multi-objective, potentially distributed, optimization algorithm for battery management. In this framework, cells or packs are treated as autonomous agents that co-optimize their local cost functions (related to wear, efficiency, and SoC) within the constraints of a global economic objective. The goal is to derive a control strategy that converges to a system-wide equilibrium, analogous to an ecological Nash equilibrium, which simultaneously minimizes total operational cost and actively drives all units to a precisely equalized State-of-Charge, thereby enhancing system longevity and performance.

ideas.title: Dynamic Imbalance-Cost Integration for Battery Fleet Optimization
ideas.description: This approach formulates battery fleet dispatch by integrating State of Charge (SoC) imbalance directly into the economic objective function. Instead of treating perfect balance as a hard constraint, it assigns a virtual 'cost of imbalance' that scales quadratically with the deviation from perfect balance across the fleet. The optimizer then minimizes the sum of this imbalance cost and the actual financial cost of energy transactions. This allows the system to automatically and rationally accept minor imbalances to avoid exorbitant energy prices or damaging battery cycles, while a tunable parameter controls the overall balance strictness.

ideas.title: Multi-Regional Stochastic Grid Coordination via Deep Reinforcement Learning with Policy-Aware Credit Transfer
ideas.description: This research develops a deep reinforcement learning framework for the stochastic, multi-timescale scheduling of a multi-regional power system with high wind penetration. The core innovation is an agent that learns to manage the complex trade-offs between diverse regional wind patterns, transmission constraints, and differing carbon pricing or regulatory regimes. By treating the interconnected system as a partially observable Markov decision process, the agent discovers adaptive strategies for reserve sharing, generation dispatch, and inter-regional power flows that explicitly hedge against both renewable forecast errors and policy-driven carbon cost uncertainties. This enables a synergistic, system-wide coordination that minimizes total cost and emissions more effectively than isolated regional optimization or traditional deterministic multi-area models.

ideas.title: Constraint-First Battery Management via Multi-Objective Optimization with Lexicographic Ordering
ideas.description: This research adapts the lexicographic ordering solution from multi-objective optimization in Operations Research to invert the priority of cost versus SoC balance in battery management systems. The analogous field solves problems with strictly hierarchical objectives by optimizing sequentially, first for the primary objective to define a feasible set, then for secondary objectives within that set. The adapted idea applies this by making perfect SoC equalization the primary, hard constraint. The control algorithm is structured in two distinct stages: the first stage's sole purpose is to find the operational envelope that achieves SoC balance, treating it as a non-negotiable requirement. The second stage then performs cost minimization, but its search is confined to the solution space that already satisfies the perfect balance condition from Stage 1. This ensures SoC health is prioritized absolutely, with economic efficiency pursued only as a secondary goal within that guaranteed balanced state.

ideas.title: Incentive-Aware Multiagent BESS Coordination via Mechanism Design and Reinforcement Learning
ideas.description: This research designs a distributed coordination system for Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) that explicitly addresses strategic behavior and private information. It combines a formally specified market mechanism to ensure truthful bidding and economic efficiency with a multi-agent deep reinforcement learning framework to enable adaptive, robust policy development under real-world uncertainties like local forecast errors and communication delays. The hybrid approach guarantees stable convergence to a near-optimal state-of-charge balance while respecting the private constraints and competitive incentives of individual BESS owners, moving beyond the idealized assumptions of traditional cooperative game theory.

ideas.title: Validate Power Grid Optimization Model via Epidemiological Backtesting
ideas.description: This research idea proposes validating a proposed power grid optimization model by adapting the backtesting methodology common in epidemiology. In epidemiology, models predicting disease spread are rigorously tested by running them with the initial conditions of a known historical outbreak and comparing their simulated progression to the actual recorded outcome. We adapt this solution by applying it to the power systems domain. The adapted idea involves obtaining a high-resolution historical dataset from an actual power system (e.g., ERCOT, PJM) containing time-series data for load, renewable generation, fuel prices, forced outages, and weather. The optimization model will be initialized with the true system state at a chosen past timestamp and then run forward in a simulation, making its own optimization decisions based on the subsequent, historically observed external inputs. The model's outputs—such as generator dispatch, locational marginal prices, reserve activation, and responses to simulated contingencies—will be quantitatively compared to the archival records of what actually occurred in the grid and markets. This backtesting approach provides a powerful, reality-grounded method to validate the model's fidelity, uncover unrealistic assumptions in its formulation, and calibrate its parameters before using it for future planning or real-time operation studies.

ideas.title: Adversarial Scenario Ensemble for Robust Grid Optimization Model Validation
ideas.description: This research develops a framework to stress-test power system optimization models beyond historical data. It uses historical data to define statistical distributions and physical relationships, then generates a wide ensemble of synthetic, future-oriented grid scenarios, including high-stress conditions like prolonged renewable droughts or correlated failures. The model is validated not by a single historical fit, but by its robust performance and the identification of its failure boundaries across this adversarial ensemble, ensuring it is prepared for real-world uncertainties.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Niche Optimization to Microgrid Resource Allocation
ideas.description: This research applies an analogy from ecology to the problem of optimizing a small-scale, isolated microgrid with local wind and storage. The analogous field is Ecology, where the solution to optimizing resource allocation in a constrained, isolated ecosystem (like an island) uses niche theory and Lotka-Volterra competition models to balance consumption and interactions for community stability. The adapted idea treats microgrid components (wind, storage, loads) as species within an energy ecosystem. By applying dynamic niche optimization models, the research will develop algorithms to schedule storage and manage generation to find the most resilient and stable operational regime, mimicking how natural ecosystems achieve balance with limited resources.

ideas.title: Adaptive Load-Balancing for Heterogeneous BESS Fleets using Ecological Succession Principles
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea by applying analogical reasoning from ecology and ecosystem management. The analogous problem is managing a heterogeneous biological population (like a forest) with individuals of varying health and maturity to sustain overall system function, solved through adaptive niche partitioning and succession role assignment. The adapted idea proposes developing EMS strategies for mixed new and second-life BESS fleets by modeling them as an 'energy ecosystem.' The EMS will implement adaptive, role-based SoC balancing, assigning tasks (e.g., long-duration storage, rapid response) to battery units based on their real-time state-of-health and degradation characteristics, much like an ecologist assigns ecological niches. This approach aims to optimize the aggregate performance and economic lifetime of the heterogeneous fleet by strategically leveraging the distinct capabilities of both new and degraded units within a unified management framework.

ideas.title: Adaptive Nonlinear EMS with Joint Parameter-State Estimation for Robust SoC Equalization
ideas.description: This research develops and validates an adaptive energy management system (EMS) that combines robust nonlinear control with real-time joint parameter and state estimation for state-of-charge (SoC) equalization. The core innovation is a dual-layer strategy where a data-driven observer continuously identifies cell-specific parameters and corrects SoC estimates under dynamic loads and aging. This information feeds into a reconfigurable nonlinear controller that adjusts its balancing strategy to the pack's actual, evolving condition. The approach is designed to maintain stable and optimal performance despite significant initial parameter uncertainty and progressive cell-to-cell heterogeneity.

ideas.title: Inverse Design of Heterogeneous Carbon Price Coordination for Multi-Regional Emission Targets
ideas.description: This research develops a framework to solve the inverse design problem for a multi-regional, interconnected power system. It determines the required carbon price trajectory for each region, which may differ due to local resource endowments and policies, such that their coordinated operation—leveraging transmission and complementary wind resources—meets a specific, system-wide emission reduction target at the lowest possible total cost. The model simultaneously optimizes generation dispatch, storage, and inter-regional power flows while back-calculating the necessary heterogeneous carbon prices, providing policymakers with a precise, cost-effective roadmap for coordinated climate action in interconnected jurisdictions.

ideas.title: Adapting Multi-Agent RL from Traffic Flow Control for Wind-Integrated Grid Scheduling
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from intelligent transportation systems, where multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) with a centralized training, decentralized execution (CTDE) paradigm is used to manage the stochastic, distributed problem of urban traffic flow control. The analogous solution coordinates multiple intersection agents under local uncertainty for a global objective. Adapted to the energy context, the research will develop a MARL framework where individual wind farms or distributed energy resource aggregators act as cooperative agents. These agents will be trained offline using a central simulator with full system information to optimize a shared objective like total operating cost or penalty minimization. For online execution, each agent uses its local policy and observations to make scheduling decisions, enabling a scalable and robust solution to the stochastic optimization of large-scale, wind-integrated power systems.

ideas.title: Safe Reinforcement Learning for Wind-Integrated Scheduling via Online Safety Verification and Projection
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid decision-making architecture for stochastic scheduling in wind-integrated power systems. A deep reinforcement learning agent learns to propose cost-effective scheduling actions by interacting with a simulated environment. Crucially, a parallel online safety-verification module, using efficient robust optimization or predictive safety filters, evaluates every proposed action against system constraints and uncertainty realizations. If unsafe, the action is minimally adjusted to a certified-safe alternative, ensuring operational reliability while allowing the RL policy to learn from corrected actions, ultimately creating an adaptive yet provably reliable scheduler.

ideas.title: Jointly Robust Scheduling for Correlated Wind and Solar Generation Under Compound Weather Risk
ideas.description: This research develops a power system scheduling model that explicitly accounts for the spatio-temporal correlations between wind and solar photovoltaic generation, driven by common weather patterns. The core innovation is the integration of a multivariate probabilistic forecast model, built using copulas or Gaussian processes, to capture the risk of concurrent low-output periods (dunkelflaute). The scheduling framework then employs risk-constrained or distributionally robust optimization to produce generation and reserve schedules that are reliable against these correlated shortfalls, thereby moving beyond simple integration to true resilience for high-renewable grids.

ideas.title: Integrating Intermittent Renewables via Multi-Source Assimilation Techniques
ideas.description: This research expands the scheduling model to include solar PV alongside wind by drawing an analogy from Numerical Weather Prediction (NWP). In NWP, data assimilation techniques like Ensemble Kalman Filters solve the problem of integrating heterogeneous, error-prone observational data from multiple sources into a coherent physical model. We adapt this solution by treating short-term forecasts for wind, solar PV, and load as uncertain 'observations' with source-specific error statistics. A data assimilation framework is then designed to optimally blend these forecasts, respecting their cross-correlations, to produce a single, most-likely scenario (or an ensemble of scenarios) that serves as the input to the security-constrained unit commitment or economic dispatch model. This approach provides a principled method for multi-renewable integration, improving schedule robustness compared to simply adding separate, uncoordinated forecast inputs.

ideas.title: A Socio-Technical, Distributionally Robust Framework for Behavioral Demand Response to Wind Power
ideas.description: This research develops a novel modeling framework that overcomes the unreliability of human-driven demand response by integrating behavioral and social dynamics into grid scheduling. An agent-based model simulates a heterogeneous population of prosumers whose participation is influenced by habits, social networks, and imperfect information. This behavioral layer generates a spectrum of possible aggregate flexibility profiles, which feed into a distributionally robust optimization model for the system operator. The final system co-optimizes energy dispatch and incentive signals to guarantee technical and economic performance even under the most adverse realistic consumer participation scenarios, creating a truly robust smart grid integration for wind power.

ideas.title: Apply Portfolio Immunization from Finance to Wind Power Scheduling
ideas.description: This idea adapts the robust optimization strategy of portfolio immunization from financial portfolio management to the problem of wind power scheduling. In finance, immunization guarantees a portfolio's value against interest rate fluctuations by matching its duration to the investment horizon under a set of yield curve shocks. The analogous solution is adapted by defining an uncertainty set for wind power forecast errors (e.g., bounded deviations, correlated across time and space) and constructing a robust optimization model for the power system dispatch. The objective is to immunize the day-ahead schedule and ancillary service procurement, ensuring that all operational constraints (power balance, line flows, ramping) remain feasible for every wind power realization within that worst-case set, thus providing a guaranteed performance guarantee against wind volatility.

ideas.title: Minimum Buffer Capacity for Fixed Network Throughput
ideas.description: This idea adapts a classic solution from queueing theory and telecommunications network design to the energy storage problem. The analogous problem is determining the minimum data buffer capacity required at network routers to guarantee a specific Quality of Service (e.g., packet loss rate) for a fixed infrastructure budget. That solution uses stochastic models of data traffic (like Poisson processes) within queuing frameworks (e.g., M/M/1/K) to derive the relationship between buffer size and performance metric, then inverts it to find the minimal sufficient buffer. Adapted to the energy context, we model the net grid load (demand minus variable renewable generation) as a stochastic arrival process to a 'storage queue.' Grid stability is defined probabilistically, such as the probability of failing to meet a power balance within a critical timeframe. For a fixed electricity system cost (which constrains the procurement and operational strategies for generation and storage power capabilities), we mathematically derive the minimum energy storage capacity (the 'buffer size' in MWh) required to keep the probability of a stability violation below a pre-specified reliability target. This transforms the original dual problem into a chance-constrained optimization or a direct inversion of a performance-reliability curve derived from stochastic models of grid operations.

ideas.title: Cyber-Physically Robust EMS for Dynamic Microgrid Topologies with Real-Time Topology Verification
ideas.description: This research develops an Energy Management System (EMS) for microgrids with dynamically reconfigurable connections of BESS units and converters, explicitly designed to withstand communication failures, delays, and cyber-physical attacks. The core innovation is a distributed, real-time topology verification engine that continuously cross-checks commanded topology changes against physical sensor measurements using graph theory and power flow consistency checks. The EMS dynamically adjusts its control strategy based on a calculated 'topology confidence index,' enabling graceful degradation from optimal, topology-aware control to robust, topology-agnostic safety modes. This ensures system stability and prevents hazardous control actions even under partial information or deception.

ideas.title: Dynamic Adaptive Robust Scheduling with Online-Learned Forecast Uncertainty Sets
ideas.description: This research develops a power system scheduling model that dynamically integrates wind forecast uncertainty. It employs online machine learning to continuously characterize prediction error distributions and construct non-conservative, data-driven uncertainty sets tailored to prevailing weather patterns. The core is a multi-stage adaptive robust optimization framework that co-optimizes day-ahead unit commitment with explicit recourse policies for real-time operations, triggered by the evolving forecast. This ensures decisions are robust against realistic, time-varying forecast errors while maintaining computational tractability and minimizing total expected cost.

ideas.title: Data-Driven Resilience Testing of Decentralized SoC Control Under Realistic Communication Faults
ideas.description: This research fuses the design of communication-resilient SoC balancing strategies with validation using real-world grid data to create a high-fidelity testing environment. The core idea is to use historical time-series data from an actual power system—including load profiles, renewable generation traces, and topology information—to generate realistic, context-aware communication fault scenarios (e.g., delays correlated with grid congestion, packet loss during severe weather events). The proposed decentralized control strategies are then rigorously evaluated within this simulated but data-grounded environment, assessing their performance not just on synthetic benchmarks but against the complex, non-stationary dynamics of a real grid. This synergy enables the discovery of failure modes and the tuning of resilience parameters that are specifically relevant to operational reality, bridging the gap between theoretical robustness and practical deployment.

ideas.title: Adaptive Scheduling for Dynamic Carbon Pricing Using Congestion Control Algorithms
ideas.description: This research investigates scheduling under time-varying or regionally differentiated carbon pricing schemes by drawing an analogy from congestion control in computer networking. The analogous solution from TCP uses feedback-driven algorithms (like AIMD) to adapt data flow to dynamic network conditions. Adapted to this context, the research will design and model scheduling algorithms for industrial or energy systems that treat the carbon price as a congestion signal. The algorithm would automatically increase or decrease carbon-intensive activities in response to price fluctuations, optimizing for total cost and stability, much like TCP optimizes for throughput and network stability, providing a novel computational framework for dynamic carbon policy compliance.

ideas.title: Data-Driven Distributionally Robust Optimization for Wind Power Integration
ideas.description: This approach applies Distributionally Robust Optimization (DRO) to manage wind uncertainty in power systems operations. It uses historical data and forecast ensembles to construct an ambiguity set of plausible probability distributions for wind power injection, rather than a simple interval-based uncertainty set. The optimization then finds a dispatch solution that is feasible under the worst-case distribution within this statistically defined set, providing a quantifiable probabilistic guarantee of reliability. This method mitigates the conservatism of classical robust optimization while maintaining rigorous protection against realistic forecast errors and tail events.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Federated Coordination for Carbon Capture and Storage Networks
ideas.description: This system employs a two-tiered architecture to coordinate carbon capture and storage infrastructure. A central coordinator uses federated learning to build a global optimization model without accessing raw local data, preserving privacy and security. It then broadcasts dynamic constraint bundles (e.g., grid load limits, storage quotas) to a decentralized layer of selfish agents that negotiate detailed operations. This hybrid approach guarantees adherence to critical system-wide limits while preserving the resilience, adaptability, and computational benefits of decentralized control.

ideas.title: Transfer distributed optimization from sensor networks
ideas.description: Adapt consensus-based distributed optimization algorithms from sensor networks for EMPC in networked microgrids.
ideas.sourceTitle: Economic Model Predictive Control for Microgrid Optimization: A Review

ideas.title: Inverse problem: Fault identification using control signals

ideas.title: Incorporate other flexible loads beyond BTESS

ideas.sourceTitle: Distributed Event-Triggered Secondary Frequency Regulation by Sharing HESS Power in Microgrids

ideas.title: Cooperative Game-Theoretic BESS Coalition Formation for Multi-Renewable Portfolio Balancing
ideas.description: This research synthesizes cooperative game theory for multi-agent BESS negotiation with the integration of multiple renewable sources (solar PV and wind) into a unified framework. It models geographically distributed BESS units as rational agents that can form dynamic coalitions, where the coalition's value (payoff) is defined by its ability to mitigate the aggregate forecast errors and variability of a specific renewable portfolio. The core innovation is a negotiation mechanism where agents bargain over coalition membership and energy schedules to maximize their shared payoff from providing portfolio-balancing services to the grid, moving beyond individual SoC balancing to optimize for the statistical complementarities and joint uncertainties of solar and wind generation.

ideas.title: Robust Multi-Regional Scheduling Under Regulatory Uncertainty and Non-Cooperation
ideas.description: This research develops optimization frameworks for interconnected regional energy systems that are robust to uncertain and volatile carbon policy landscapes. It treats future policies as stochastic variables and models regions as potential non-cooperative actors with divergent objectives. The core methodology generates scheduling strategies that maintain economic and reliability performance across a wide range of plausible policy futures, including sudden regional policy reversals. The outcome is a decision-support system that enables resilient investment and dispatch in the face of regulatory instability.

ideas.title: Scale up: Continental-scale EV fleet dispatch
ideas.description: Investigate algorithms for coordinating millions of EVs across a continent for bulk energy and frequency services.
ideas.sourceTitle: Power System Integration of Electric Vehicles: A Review on Impacts and Contributions to the Smart Grid

ideas.title: Dynamic, Self-Organizing Agent Clustering Inspired by Protein Folding
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea of refining agent categorization by drawing an analogy from computational protein folding. In that field, the complex problem of predicting a protein's functional 3D structure from its linear amino acid sequence is solved by modeling pairwise interaction potentials (e.g., hydrophobic, electrostatic) and using them to guide dynamic, energy-minimizing folding into stable domains. We adapt this solution to cluster-based multiagent systems for large Microgrids (MGs). Here, each agent is assigned a profile based on attributes like its primary function (generation, storage, load), communication frequency, and physical/cyber locality. A defined interaction potential function, quantifying the 'cost' or 'benefit' of agent pairing, allows the system to perform continuous, lightweight computation. Agents then dynamically self-organize into clusters by minimizing the total system interaction energy, leading to emergent, optimal groupings that enhance scalability and communication efficiency without centralized, pre-defined categories. This enables the system to adapt fluidly to changes in MG topology, agent population, and operational states.

ideas.title: Challenge uniform EV availability for grid services

ideas.title: Adaptive Microgrid Control via Mobile Ad-Hoc Network Protocols
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of a static microgrid topology by developing EMS strategies for microgrids where BESS units and converters can be dynamically connected or disconnected. The analogy is drawn from Mobile Ad-Hoc Networks (MANETs) in wireless communications, which solved the problem of dynamic node connectivity with self-organizing, on-demand routing protocols like AODV. Adapting this solution, the research proposes a distributed EMS that treats power assets as autonomous nodes. These nodes would use a lightweight communication layer to continuously advertise their state and discover peer nodes, enabling the microgrid to autonomously form, optimize, and reconfigure power flow paths and control actions in real-time as the physical and electrical topology changes, ensuring resilience and efficiency.

ideas.title: Hybrid Predictive-Swarm Control for Real-Time Decentralized SoC Management
ideas.description: This redesigned system addresses the fatal flaw of unpredictable convergence by decoupling timescales of control. A lower layer of distributed model-predictive controllers (MPCs) at each node ensures deterministic, fast-response stability for real-time power balancing. An upper layer employs a constrained particle swarm optimization (PSO) algorithm that operates asynchronously, using system-wide telemetry to meta-optimize the cost functions and constraint boundaries of the local MPCs. This creates a stable, emergent adaptation to changing network topologies and component degradation, while guaranteeing real-time performance through the proven local controllers.

ideas.title: A Homeostatic Grid: Modeling Flexible Loads as a Physiological Buffer for Wind Power
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of passive, inelastic demand by proposing a homeostatic model for grid balance, inspired by physiological regulation. In human physiology, homeostasis maintains stability through antagonistic effectors and negative feedback (e.g., insulin and glucagon regulating blood glucose). The analogous solution is adapted by modeling flexible loads as two opposing classes: one that increases consumption to absorb surplus wind power ('insulin-like' response) and one that decreases consumption or provides stored energy during deficits ('glucagon-like' response). A control system, using wind forecast error and grid frequency as its feedback signal, dynamically activates these demand-side resources to create a self-balancing, buffer-like effect that mitigates wind power intermittency, mimicking the robustness of a biological system.

ideas.title: Scale down to a single microgrid community
